第一章 — THE JAPANESE SOUND SYSTEM
Ch 1: Sounds & Hiragana · 42 vocabulary items
CHAPTER 1 VOCABULARY — USEFUL EXPRESSIONS & CLASSROOM LANGUAGE
JapaneseRomajiTypeEnglishNotes
せんせいsenseiNounteacher, professorCh1; title of respect; never use for yourself
~さん~sanSuffixMr./Mrs./Miss/Ms.Ch1; generic polite title; never use for yourself
~せんせい~senseiSuffixProfessor ~Ch1; used as a title with names
はじめましてhajimemashiteExpressionHow do you do?Ch1; first meeting greeting
~です~desuCopulaI am ~Ch1; polite copula
どうぞよろしくdouzo yoroshikuExpressionPleased to meet youCh1; after self-introduction
おはようohayouExpressionGood morning (casual)Ch1; casual — use ございます with superiors
おはようございますohayou gozaimasuExpressionGood morning (formal)Ch1; for teachers/superiors/strangers
こんにちはkonnichi waExpressionGood afternoon / HelloCh1; ⚠️ は pronounced わ; not used with family
こんばんはkonban waExpressionGood evening / HelloCh1; ⚠️ は pronounced わ; not used with family
さようなら / さよならsayounara / sayonaraExpressionGood-byeCh1; formal farewell
しつれいしますshitsurei shimasuExpressionGood-bye / Excuse meCh1; lit. "I will be rude"; polite leave-taking
じゃあ、またjaa, mataExpressionSee you laterCh1; casual; lit. "well then, again"
(あのう、)すみません(anou,) sumimasenExpression(Um,) Excuse me / I am sorryCh1; attention-getting or apology
ありがとうございますarigatou gozaimasuExpressionThank youCh1; polite thanks
どういたしましてdou itashimashiteExpressionYou are welcomeCh1; response to thanks
いいえ、わかりませんiie, wakarimasenExpressionNo, I don't understandCh1
はい、わかりましたhai, wakarimashitaExpressionYes, I understoodCh1
いってくださいitte kudasaiExpressionPlease say it / Repeat after meCh1; instructor request
おおきいこえでいってくださいookii koe de itte kudasaiExpressionPlease speak loudlyCh1; instructor request form
おおきいこえでおねがいしますookii koe de onegai shimasuExpressionPlease speak loudlyCh1; student request form
かいてくださいkaite kudasaiExpressionPlease writeCh1
きいてくださいkiite kudasaiExpressionPlease listenCh1
みてくださいmite kudasaiExpressionPlease look at itCh1
よんでくださいyonde kudasaiExpressionPlease readCh1
もういちどいってくださいmou ichido itte kudasaiExpressionPlease say it againCh1; instructor request
もういちどおねがいしますmou ichido onegai shimasuExpressionPlease say it againCh1; student request
もうすこしゆっくりおねがいしますmou sukoshi yukkuri onegai shimasuExpressionPlease speak more slowlyCh1; student request
わかりましたかwakarimashita kaExpressionDo you understand?Ch1; teacher comprehension check
これはにほんごでなんといいますかkore wa nihongo de nan to iimasu kaExpressionHow do you say this in Japanese?Ch1; これ = near speaker
それはにほんごでなんといいますかsore wa nihongo de nan to iimasu kaExpressionHow do you say that in Japanese?Ch1; それ = near listener
あれはにほんごでなんといいますかare wa nihongo de nan to iimasu kaExpressionHow do you say that (over there) in Japanese?Ch1; あれ = far from both
~はにほんごでなんといいますか~ wa nihongo de nan to iimasu kaExpressionHow do you say ~ in Japanese?Ch1; general pattern
~ってなんですか~ tte nan desu kaExpressionWhat does ~ mean?Ch1; casual meaning question
~といいます / ~っていいます~ to iimasu / ~ tte iimasuExpressionYou say ~ / You call it ~Ch1; って is casual form of と
JAPANESE WRITING SYSTEM OVERVIEW
KEY TERM: The "Three-Headed Monster" (Orthographic System)

Japanese uses three scripts simultaneously:
1. Kanji (漢字): Chinese characters used for content words (nouns, verb stems). Represents meaning.
2. Hiragana (ひらがな): Native phonetic script for grammar, particles, and native words. Evolved from simplified Chinese characters used purely for sound (onna de / women's hand).
3. Katakana (カタカナ): Phonetic script for foreign loanwords and onomatopoeia.

Mechanism: The Japanese initially borrowed Chinese characters based on meaning, then half-assed it (adapted it phonetically) to write their own polysyllabic language, eventually simplifying the strokes into Hiragana.
⚠️ TRAP: Thinking Hiragana characters have intrinsic meanings. They do NOT. They are purely phonetic syllables (morae).
WRITING SYSTEM SCRIPT SELECTOR
What are you writing?
↓
カタカナ
Foreign loanwords
コーヒー
coffee
Foreign names
マイク
Mike
Onomatopoeia
ワンワン
woof woof
Emphasis (like CAPS)
ダメ！
No way!
ひらがな
Grammar particles
は、が、を、に
topic, subject, object, etc.
Verb/adj endings
たべます
conjugation suffixes
Native words (no kanji)
おはよう
good morning
Kanji readings (furigana)
食たべる
pronunciation guide
漢字
Content words (meaning)
山 = やま
mountain
Verb/adj stems
食べる
eat (stem = 食)
Compound words
大学 = だいがく
big+study = university
Multiple readings
人 = ひと / じん / にん
person (3 readings!)
Quick Rule: Foreign word? → カタカナ  |  Grammar/function? → ひらがな  |  Meaning/content? → 漢字

1. What Is It

Japanese is the only major language that mixes three completely different writing systems in a single sentence. Kanji carry meaning, Hiragana handle grammar and native words, and Katakana mark foreign imports — and you need all three from day one.

2. Rules Table — When to Use Each Script

ScriptUse ForExampleWhy This Script
KanjiNouns, verb/adj stems食べる (to eat)Kanji 食 = "eat" meaning
KanjiNumbers & counters三人 (3 people)Kanji compress info visually
HiraganaParticlesは、が、を、にGrammar glue = always hiragana
HiraganaVerb/adj endings食べますConjugation parts change = hiragana
HiraganaNative words w/o kanjiおいしい (delicious)Some words simply don't use kanji
HiraganaFurigana (reading aid)漢字かんじTiny hiragana above kanji for pronunciation
KatakanaForeign loanwordsコーヒー (coffee)Signals "this word came from abroad"
KatakanaOnomatopoeiaドキドキ (heartbeat)Sound effects stand out in katakana
KatakanaForeign namesマイク (Mike)Your name in Japanese = katakana
KatakanaEmphasis / slangマジで (seriously)Like CAPS LOCK or italics in English

3. Contrast Examples — Same Sentence, Different Scripts

SentenceScript BreakdownWhy Each Part Uses That Script
アメリカ人です。 アメリカ人です。 Katakana = foreign word (America) | Kanji = meaning (person) | Hiragana = grammar (copula)
コーヒーをのみます。 コーヒーをのみます。 Katakana = loanword (coffee) | Hiragana = particle + verb

4. Mnemonic

KHK = "Kanji Holds Knowledge, Hiragana Holds Grammar, Katakana Holds Imports"
Think of a Japanese sentence as a sandwich: Kanji = the meat (content), Hiragana = the bread (holds everything together), Katakana = the foreign cheese (imported topping).

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Writing foreign words in hiragana.
❌ WRONG: こーひー (coffee in hiragana)
✅ RIGHT: コーヒー (coffee in katakana)
Foreign loanwords MUST use katakana.
⚠️ TRAP #2: Writing particles in katakana.
❌ WRONG: コーヒーヲのみます
✅ RIGHT: コーヒーをのみます
Particles are ALWAYS hiragana, no exceptions.
⚠️ TRAP #3: Confusing は the particle with は the hiragana.
❌ WRONG: Pronouncing は as "ha" in わたしはがくせいです
✅ RIGHT: Pronouncing は as "wa" when it is the topic particle.
は = "wa" ONLY as topic particle. Otherwise it is "ha" (e.g., はな = flower).

6. Decision Tree — "Which Script Do I Use?"

Is the word from a foreign language?
YES ↓
Use KATAKANA
カタカナ
NO ↓
Is it a particle or grammar word?
YES ↓
Use HIRAGANA
ひらがな
NO ↓
Is there a kanji you know?
YES ↓
Use KANJI
漢字
NO ↓
Use HIRAGANA
ひらがな

7. Cheat Sheet

ScriptLooks LikeUsed ForCountLearn By
HiraganaCurvy, roundedGrammar, particles, native words46 basicCh 1-2
KatakanaAngular, sharpForeign words, emphasis, sounds46 basicCh 1-2
KanjiComplex, denseContent words (nouns, verb stems)2000+ (learn ~50 by Ch 6)Ongoing
HIRAGANA: VOWELS & K, S, T, N, H ROWS
KEY TERM: The Motherfuckin' Basics (Fundamental Morae)

Japanese sounds are based on 5 vowels: a, i, u, e, o.
- a: short "ah"
- i: short "ee"
- u: short "oo" (lips relaxed, not rounded)
- e: short "eh"
- o: short "oh"

Consonants attach to these. Note the irregulars:
- S-row: し is shi (not si)
- T-row: ち is chi (not ti), つ is tsu (not tu)
- H-row: ふ is fu (lips together as if blowing out a candle, not quite "hu" or "fu")
⚠️ TRAP: Pronouncing "u" like the English "ooh" with rounded lips. Japanese "u" is flat/relaxed.
HIRAGANA GRID (A to HO)
aiueo
-あいうえお
kかきくけこ
sさし (shi)すせそ
tたち (chi)つ (tsu)てと
nなにぬねの
hはひふ (fu)へほ
Red = irregular romanization (shi not si, chi not ti, tsu not tu, fu not hu)

1. What Is It

Hiragana is the foundational phonetic alphabet of Japanese — 46 base characters representing every possible syllable (mora) in the language. Master these first, because every Japanese word can be written in hiragana.

2. Rules Table — The 5 Vowels: Mouth Shape Guide

VowelHiraganaSoundMouth ShapeEnglish ApproximationCommon Mistake
aあ"ah"Mouth wide open, jaw dropped"a" in "father"Saying "ay" like in "cake"
iい"ee"Lips pulled wide, teeth close"ee" in "see" (but shorter)Making it too long/diphthong
uう"oo"Lips RELAXED & slightly forward, NOT roundedBetween "oo" and "uh"Rounding lips like English "ooh"
eえ"eh"Mouth half-open, tongue mid-front"e" in "pet"Saying "ee" or diphthong "ay"
oお"oh"Lips slightly rounded (less than English)"o" in "go" (no glide)Adding a "w" glide: "ow"

Stroke Order Rules

RuleMeaningExample
Top to BottomStart from the top of the characterえ — horizontal stroke first, then the lower parts
Left to RightWrite left strokes before right strokesい — left curve, then right curve
Horizontal before VerticalWhen strokes cross, horizontal goes firstた — horizontal, then vertical, then the other strokes
Center before WingsMiddle vertical first when flankedこ — top horizontal, then bottom

3. Contrast Examples — Irregular Sounds

ExpectedActualWhy
si → しshiS-row irregularity: tongue position creates "sh" sound
ti → ちchiT-row irregularity: tongue hits palate differently before "i"
tu → つtsuT-row irregularity: air is squeezed out as a fricative
hu → ふfuH-row irregularity: lips come close (blowing out a candle, not biting lip)

4. Mnemonic

"Ah, we (i) ooze (u) energy (e) oh!" — a-i-u-e-o
The 5 vowels in order. Every consonant row follows this same vowel order: ka-ki-ku-ke-ko, sa-shi-su-se-so, etc.

Irregulars mnemonic: "She Chews TSUnami FUel"
Shi, Chi, Tsu, Fu — the four sounds that break the consonant+vowel pattern.

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Rounding lips for Japanese "u"
❌ WRONG: Puckering lips like English "oo" in "food"
✅ RIGHT: Keep lips relaxed, slightly parted, air flows naturally
Japanese う is "unrounded" — think of it as a lazy "oo".
⚠️ TRAP #2: Confusing similar-looking hiragana
❌ き (ki) vs さ (sa) — both have horizontal strokes but き has 4 strokes, さ has 3
❌ は (ha) vs ほ (ho) — ほ has an extra right-side stroke
❌ ね (ne) vs れ (re) — ね loops back, れ flicks out
⚠️ TRAP #3: Adding vowel glides (diphthongs)
❌ WRONG: Saying "oh" as "oh-oo" or "eh" as "eh-ee"
✅ RIGHT: Each vowel is ONE pure, short, flat sound. No gliding.

7. Cheat Sheet — Rows A through H

RowaiueoWatch Out
Vowelsあいうえおう = relaxed lips!
Kかきくけこ—
Sさし shiすせそし not "si"
Tたち chiつ tsuてと2 irregulars!
Nなにぬねの—
Hはひふ fuへほふ = blow candle
HIRAGANA: M, Y, R, W ROWS & N
KEY TERM: The Weirdos (Irregular Rows)

- Y-row: Only 3 sounds: や (ya), ゆ (yu), よ (yo). "yi" and "ye" do not exist.
- R-row: The Japanese "r" is a flap sound. It's a bastard child of English r, l, and d. Flick the tip of the tongue against the gum behind the upper teeth.
- W-row: Only わ (wa) and を (wo). を is pronounced exactly like "o" and is ONLY used as a particle marking the direct object.
- N: ん (n) is the only standalone consonant. It never starts a word. Pronunciation shifts slightly depending on the following consonant (m before m/p/b, ng before k/g).
⚠️ TRAP: Pronouncing "r" like the hard American "R". It is a light flick, much closer to the "tt" in "butter".
HIRAGANA GRID (MA to N)
aiueo
mまみむめも
yや—ゆ—よ
rらりるれろ
wわ———を (particle)
nん — standalone consonant, never starts a word
を is pronounced "o" but written with w-row. R-row sounds like "tt" in "butter".
VOICED CONSONANTS (DAKUTEN / HANDAKUTEN)
KEY TERM: Add "Quotes" to vibrate (Voicing)

Voiceless consonants become voiced by adding two dots ゛(dakuten). Your vocal chords vibrate.
- k → g (か ka → が ga)
- s → z (さ sa → ざ za). Note: し (shi) → じ (ji).
- t → d (た ta → だ da). Note: ち (chi) → ぢ (ji), つ (tsu) → づ (zu).
- h → b (は ha → ば ba).

KEY TERM: Add a "Hole" to pop (P-sounds)
Add a small circle ゜(handakuten) to the H-row ONLY to make a "p" sound.
- h → p (は ha → ぱ pa).
⚠️ TRAP: Confusing じ (ji) and ぢ (ji), or ず (zu) and づ (zu). They sound identical. じ and ず are used 95% of the time.
DAKUTEN & HANDAKUTEN — COMPLETE VOICING CHART
゛ Dakuten (Voicing)
K → G
か(ka)→が(ga)   き→ぎ   く→ぐ   け→げ   こ→ご
S → Z
さ(sa)→ざ(za)   し→じ(ji)   す→ず   せ→ぜ   そ→ぞ
T → D
た(ta)→だ(da)   ち→ぢ(ji)   つ→づ(zu)   て→で   と→ど
H → B
は(ha)→ば(ba)   ひ→び   ふ→ぶ   へ→べ   ほ→ぼ
゜ Handakuten (H→P only)
H → P
は(ha)→ぱ(pa)
ひ(hi)→ぴ(pi)
ふ(fu)→ぷ(pu)
へ(he)→ぺ(pe)
ほ(ho)→ぽ(po)
Duplicate sounds:
じ (ji) = ぢ (ji) — use じ 95%
ず (zu) = づ (zu) — use ず 95%

1. What Is It

Dakuten (゛) and handakuten (゜) are tiny marks added to hiragana to change voiceless consonants into voiced ones. Think of it like adding vibration to your throat — "ka" becomes "ga," "sa" becomes "za," and so on. Handakuten only applies to the H-row, turning "ha" into "pa."

2. Rules Table — Complete Dakuten/Handakuten Grid

Base RowMarkResultaiueo
K → G゛Voicedが gaぎ giぐ guげ geご go
S → Z゛Voicedざ zaじ jiず zuぜ zeぞ zo
T → D゛Voicedだ daぢ jiづ zuで deど do
H → B゛Voicedば baび biぶ buべ beぼ bo
H → P゜Poppedぱ paぴ piぷ puぺ peぽ po

3. Contrast Examples — Voiceless vs Voiced Minimal Pairs

VoicelessMeaningVoicedMeaningOnly Difference
かみ (kami)paper / godがみ (gami)(in compounds: origami)k → g
さけ (sake)alcoholざけ (zake)(in compounds)s → z
たま (tama)ball / gemだま (dama)(in compounds: daruma)t → d
はし (hashi)bridge / chopsticksばし (bashi)(in compounds)h → b

4. Mnemonic

"Dakuten = Double dots = Deeper voice"
The two dots (゛) make the sound deeper/louder. Put your hand on your throat: voiceless (k, s, t, h) = no vibration. Voiced (g, z, d, b) = vibration.

"Handakuten = Half circle = Half the H → Pop!"
The little circle (゜) looks like a bubble — and "p" sounds literally pop air out of your lips. Only works on H-row.

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: じ vs ぢ — identical pronunciation!
Both say "ji" but じ is used 95% of the time.
ぢ only appears in:
• Repeated sounds: ちぢむ (to shrink) — ち followed by its voiced pair
• Compound words: はなぢ (nosebleed) — はな + ち
✅ DEFAULT: When in doubt, use じ.
⚠️ TRAP #2: ず vs づ — identical pronunciation!
Both say "zu" but ず is the default.
づ only appears in:
• Repeated sounds: つづく (to continue) — つ + its voiced pair
• Compound words: みかづき (crescent moon) — つき → づき
✅ DEFAULT: When in doubt, use ず.
⚠️ TRAP #3: Adding dakuten to rows that don't have them
❌ WRONG: Adding ゛ to N-row, M-row, R-row, or W-row
✅ RIGHT: Dakuten ONLY works on K, S, T, H rows. Handakuten ONLY on H-row.

6. Decision Tree — "Which Mark Do I Add?"

Is the base character from K, S, T, or H row?
NO ↓
No marks possible
N/M/R/W/Y rows
YES ↓
Do you want a P-sound?
YES ↓
Is it H-row?
YES
Add ゜
NO
Not possible!
NO ↓
Add ゛ (dakuten)
K→G, S→Z, T→D, H→B

7. Cheat Sheet

TransformationMarkRows AffectedRemember
Voiceless → Voiced゛ (dakuten / "tenten")K→G, S→Z, T→D, H→BHand on throat = feel vibration
H → P゜ (handakuten / "maru")H-row ONLYCircle = bubble → pop
じ vs ぢBoth = "ji"Default: じぢ only after ち in compounds
ず vs づBoth = "zu"Default: ずづ only after つ in compounds
GLIDES, LONG VOWELS, & DOUBLE CONSONANTS
KEY TERM: Sneaky Bastards (Modifiers)

1. Glides (Semi-vowels): Combine an i-row character with a SMALL ゃ, ゅ, ょ (ya, yu, yo).
- きゃ (kya), しゅ (shu), ちょ (cho), びゃ (bya), ぴゅ (pyu).
- Note: しや (shiya) is 2 beats. しゃ (sha) is 1 beat.

2. Double Consonants: A SMALL っ (tsu) indicates a glottal stop. Hold the consonant for one full extra beat.
- きって (kitte) = stamp. Not "kite".

3. Long Vowels: Stretch the vowel for an extra beat.
- Add あ, い, う, え, お. - Exception: "oo" is usually written with う (e.g., こうこう kookoo), and "ee" is usually written with い (e.g., せんせい sensee).
⚠️ TRAP: Ignoring the small っ. さか (saka = hill) vs さっか (sakka = writer). Completely different words.
TIMING (MORAE) — HOW JAPANESE RHYTHM WORKS
Glides (Combined Sounds)
Small kana = 1 beat
しゃ (sha) = 1 beat
Full kana = 2 beats
しや (shiya) = 1 2
All combos:
きゃ きゅ きょ / しゃ しゅ しょ
ちゃ ちゅ ちょ / にゃ にゅ にょ
ひゃ ひゅ ひょ / みゃ みゅ みょ
りゃ りゅ りょ / ぎゃ ぎゅ ぎょ
Doubles & Long Vowels
Small っ = pause (1 beat)
きって (kitte) = ki!te
(3 beats, middle = glottal stop)
Long vowels = hold 2 beats
おとうさん = o-to-o-sa-n
(5 beats, the う extends と)
Long vowel rules:
aa → add あ   ii → add い
uu → add う   ee → usually い
oo → usually う (not お)

1. What Is It

Japanese has three "modifier" mechanics that change how sounds behave: combined sounds (glides), double consonants (small っ), and long vowels. These affect timing (mora count) and can completely change a word's meaning.

2. Rules Table — Combined Sound Rules

FeatureHow It WorksSymbolBeat CountExample
Glide (combined)i-column char + small ゃ/ゅ/ょSmall ya/yu/yo1 mora (fused)きゃ (kya) = 1 beat
NOT a glidei-column char + FULL-SIZE や/ゆ/よBig ya/yu/yo2 morae (separate)きや (kiya) = 2 beats
Double consonantSmall っ before a consonantSmall tsu1 silent mora (pause)きっぷ (kippu) = 3 beats
Long vowel (a)Add あ—+1 moraおかあさん (okaasan)
Long vowel (i)Add い—+1 moraおにいさん (oniisan)
Long vowel (u)Add う—+1 moraくうき (kuuki)
Long vowel (e)Usually add い (not え)—+1 moraせんせい (sensee)
Long vowel (o)Usually add う (not お)—+1 moraおとうさん (otousan)

3. Contrast Examples — Minimal Pairs

Without ModifierMeaningWith ModifierMeaningWhat Changed
おばさん (obasan)auntおばあさん (obaasan)grandmotherLong vowel (aa)
おじさん (ojisan)uncleおじいさん (ojiisan)grandfatherLong vowel (ii)
きて (kite)come!きって (kitte)stamp / cut!Double consonant
びよういん (biyouin)beauty salonびょういん (byouin)hospitalGlide (yo vs i-yo)

4. Mnemonic

"Small = Squeeze"
Every time you see a small character, something gets squeezed:
• Small ゃゅょ = squeeze two sounds into one beat (glide)
• Small っ = squeeze a pause before the next consonant (double consonant)

Think of small characters as "shrunk down" because they lost their independence.

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Mixing up long "o" and long "e" spelling
❌ WRONG: せんせえ for "sensee"
✅ RIGHT: せんせい — long "e" is almost always spelled with い, not え
❌ WRONG: おとおさん for "otousan"
✅ RIGHT: おとうさん — long "o" is almost always spelled with う, not お
⚠️ TRAP #2: Skipping the small っ pause
❌ WRONG: Saying いった (itta = said) as "ita"
✅ RIGHT: Hold the "t" sound for one full beat: "it-ta"
The small っ is a FULL mora of silence/held consonant.

7. Cheat Sheet

ModifierLooks LikeDoes WhatKey Rule
Glidei-char + small ゃゅょFuses 2 chars into 1 beatSMALL ya/yu/yo only
Double consonantsmall っAdds 1 beat of held consonantOnly before k, s, t, p
Long vowelExtra vowel characterStretches vowel 1 more beat"ee" = い, "oo" = う (usually)
GREETINGS & DAILY EXPRESSIONS
KEY TERM: Time-based Ass-Kissing (Formal Greetings)

- Morning: おはよう ございます (ohayoo gozaimasu). Casual: おはよう.
- Afternoon: こんにちは (konnichi wa).
- Evening: こんばんは (konban wa).

Apologies / Thanks:
- Thank you: ありがとう ございます (arigatoo gozaimasu).
- You're welcome: どういたしまして (doo itashimashite).
- Excuse me / I'm sorry: すみません (sumimasen).

Taking Leave:
- To professor/superior: しつれい します (shitsuree shimasu = "I am about to commit a rudeness").
- To friends: じゃあ、また (jaa, mata = "See you later").
- Won't see for a while: さようなら (sayoonara).
⚠️ TRAP: Writing こんにちは as こんにちわ. The "wa" is the topic particle, so it is written with は (ha). Same for こんばんは.
GREETING DECISION TREE
What time is it? + Who are you talking to?
↓
☀ MORNING
Formal (teacher/boss)
おはようございます
"Good morning" (polite)
Casual (friends)
おはよう
"Morning!"
☁ AFTERNOON
All formality levels
こんにちは
Ends with は (ha) not わ!
☾ EVENING
All formality levels
こんばんは
Ends with は (ha) not わ!
GOODBYE — Match to Relationship
Superior/Prof
しつれい します
"Pardon my rudeness"
Friends
じゃあ、また
"See ya!"
Long absence
さようなら
"Farewell"
CLASSROOM SURVIVAL PHRASES
KEY TERM: Survival Cheat Codes (Instructional Commands)

Teacher Requests (〜て ください):
- きいて ください (kiite kudasai) = Please listen.
- みて ください (mite kudasai) = Please look.
- かいて ください (kaite kudasai) = Please write.
- よんで ください (yonde kudasai) = Please read.
- いって ください (itte kudasai) = Please say it.

Student Requests (〜おねがいします):
- もう いちど おねがいします = Once more, please.
- おおきい こえで おねがいします = Loud voice, please.
- もう すこし ゆっくり おねがいします = A bit more slowly, please.

Asking "How do you say...?":
- 〜は にほんごで なんと いいますか。 = How do you say ~ in Japanese?
⚠️ TRAP: Using 〜てください to a teacher. It is a command form. Use おねがいします (onegaishimasu) instead to be polite to superiors.
CLASSROOM DYNAMICS — WHO SAYS WHAT
TEACHER → Uses 〜てください
きいてください
Please listen
みてください
Please look
かいてください
Please write
よんでください
Please read
いってください
Please say it
STUDENT → Uses おねがいします
もういちど おねがいします
Once more, please
ゆっくり おねがいします
Slowly, please
おおきいこえで おねがいします
Loud voice, please
Comprehension check:
T: わかりましたか。
S: はい、わかりました。
S: いいえ、わかりません。
第二章 — GREETINGS & INTRODUCTIONS
Ch 2: Introductions · 94 vocabulary items
CHAPTER 2 VOCABULARY — GREETINGS & INTRODUCTIONS
JapaneseRomajiTypeEnglishNotes
アジアけんきゅうAjia kenkyuuNounAsian studiesCh2; major
アメリカAmerikaNounAmerica, the United StatesCh2; country
イギリスIgirisuNounEnglandCh2; country
いちねんせいichinenseiNounfreshman, first-year studentCh2; suffix せい may be dropped
いまimaNounnowCh2; no に particle needed
えいごeigoNounEnglish (language)Ch2

ぼくbokuPronounI (male)Ch2; ⚠️ normally used by males only
わたしwatashiPronounICh2; used by both males and females
ですdesuCopula(to) beCh2; polite copula
いちじichijiNounone o'clockCh2; uses に particle
にじnijiNountwo o'clockCh2
さんじsanjiNounthree o'clockCh2

じゅうにじjuunijiNountwelve o'clockCh2

どこdokoQuestion wordwhereCh2
どちらdochiraQuestion wordwhere (polite), which wayCh2; more polite than どこ
なに / なんnani / nanQuestion wordwhatCh2; なん before で, じ, にん etc.
かkaParticlequestion markerCh2; sentence-final question
のnoParticlenoun modifier (of / 's)Ch2; AのB = A's B
はwaParticletopic markerCh2; ⚠️ written は pronounced わ
もmoParticlealso, tooCh2; replaces は/が/を for similarity
お~o~Prefixpolite prefixCh2; e.g. おなまえ
~ご~goSuffixlanguageCh2; e.g. にほんご
~じ~jiSuffix~ o'clockCh2; counter for hours
~じん~jinSuffixnationalityCh2; e.g. アメリカじん
~せい~seiSuffixstudentCh2; e.g. だいがくせい
~ねん~nenSuffixyearCh2; e.g. いちねん
あのうanouInterjectionuh, well...Ch2; hesitation filler
いいえiieInterjectionno; don't mention it; you're welcomeCh2
はい / ええhai / eeInterjectionyesCh2; ええ is slightly softer

ええ / はい、そうですee / hai, sou desuExpressionYes, that's soCh2; polite affirmation
~からきました~ kara kimashitaExpressioncame from ~Ch2; casual origin
こちらこそkochira kosoExpressionSame hereCh2; reciprocal thanks
そうですかsou desu kaExpressionIs that so? I see.Ch2; aizuchi
~っていいます~ tte iimasuExpressioncolloquial of ~といいますCh2
どうもありがとうございますdoumo arigatou gozaimasuExpressionThank you very muchCh2; very polite
どこからきましたかdoko kara kimashita kaExpressionWhere are you from?Ch2
どちらからいらっしゃいましたかdochira kara irasshaimashita kaExpressionWhere are you from? (polite)Ch2; ⚠️ honorific of くる
GRAMMAR: X wa Y desu (Identity & Negation)
KEY TERM: The "A equals B" Equation (Copula Sentence)

Plain English: This is the simplest way to say "X is Y" in Japanese. The word です works like the English "am/is/are." The particle は (pronounced "wa") flags the topic — what you're talking about.

Pattern: [Topic X] は [Noun Y] です。
"As for X, it is Y."

- わたしは がくせいです。 (watashi wa gakusei desu) — I am a student.
- たなかさんは さんねんせいです。 (Tanaka-san wa sannensei desu) — Tanaka is a junior.

Negative Form: Replace です with じゃありません (ja arimasen) = "is NOT."
- わたしは せんせい じゃありません。 (watashi wa sensei ja arimasen) — I am not a teacher.

Common mistake: わたしは たなかさんです → わたしは たなかです (Don't use ~さん for yourself!)
🧠 Memory trick: は (wa) = the topic spotlight. です (desu) = "equals." So every sentence is: [spotlight on X] = [Y]. Done!
⚠️ TRAP: Pronouncing the topic marker は as "ha". When used as a particle, it is ALWAYS pronounced "wa". (But still written as は). In words like はし (chopsticks), it IS "ha."
COPULA SENTENCE STRUCTURE — ALL 4 FORMS
✅ AFFIRMATIVE
Present: X は Y です
たなかさんは がくせいです。
"Tanaka is a student."
Past: X は Y でした
たなかさんは がくせいでした。
"Tanaka was a student."
❌ NEGATIVE
Present: X は Y じゃありません
すずきさんは せんせいじゃありません。
"Suzuki is not a teacher."
Past: X は Y じゃありませんでした
すずきさんは せんせいじゃありませんでした。
"Suzuki was not a teacher."
Structure: [Topic] は [Comment] です/じゃありません   |   は always pronounced "wa"
GRAMMAR: QUESTIONS & QUESTION WORDS
KEY TERM: Slap a か on the end (Interrogative Particle)

Plain English: Making a question in Japanese is the easiest thing ever. Take any statement and add か at the end. Done. No word rearranging needed. か = spoken question mark.

Yes/No Questions:
- キムさんは かんこくじんですか。 (Kimu-san wa kankokujin desu ka) — Is Kim Korean?
- Answer: はい、そうです。 (Yes, that's right) / いいえ、ちがいます。 (No, that's wrong)

Question Words: Replace the unknown info with a question word. The word goes WHERE THE ANSWER WOULD GO.
- なん (nan): What. せんこうは なんですか。 (What is your major?)
- どこ (doko): Where. どこからですか。 (Where are you from?)
- だれ (dare): Who. あのひとは だれですか。 (Who is that person?)

Common mistake: なん ですか せんこうは → せんこうは なんですか (Question words do NOT go at the beginning like English!)
🧠 Memory trick: In Japanese, questions keep the SAME word order as answers. The question word sits in the EXACT spot where the answer goes. "Major は English です" → "Major は なん ですか." Just swap the answer for the question word!
⚠️ TRAP: Putting question words at the beginning like English. Japanese word order stays the same for questions — just add か or swap in a question word.
QUESTION FORMATION — TWO METHODS
Yes/No: Add か
Statement → Question
キムさんは かんこくじんです。
↓ Just add か
キムさんは かんこくじんですか。
"Is Kim Korean?"
Word order stays SAME!
No inversion like English.
Rising intonation optional.
WH-: Swap answer for question word
Answer → Question
せんこうは えいご です。
↓ Swap answer for なん
せんこうは なん ですか。
"What is your major?"
Common question words:
なん/なに=what   だれ=who
どこ=where   いつ=when
なんじ=what time   なんさい=how old
GRAMMAR: THE PARTICLE の (NO)
KEY TERM: The Ownership Hook (Noun Modifier)

Plain English: の is the glue between two nouns. It can mean "'s" (possession), "of" (belonging), or "about" (topic). The modifier goes FIRST, the main thing goes SECOND. Think of it backwards from English: "my book" = わたしの ほん (literally: "me's book").

Pattern: [Noun 1] の [Noun 2] — Noun 1 describes/owns Noun 2

1. Possession: (like "'s")
- わたしの せんこう (watashi no senkou) — my major
- せんせいの ほん (sensei no hon) — teacher's book

2. Affiliation: (like "of" or "at")
- とうきょうだいがくの がくせい — student of/at Tokyo University
- メキシコの うち — house in Mexico

3. Description: (like "about")
- にほんごの ほん — book about Japanese / Japanese language book
- ホストファミリーの すずきさん — Mr. Suzuki of the host family
🧠 Memory trick: の = "'s" or "of." The main noun is ALWAYS last. Read Japanese right-to-left to translate: にほんご の ほん → "book OF Japanese" = Japanese book. Details first, main thing last!
⚠️ TRAP: の only connects NOUNS to NOUNS. You CANNOT use の with verbs. WRONG: たべるの ごはん. Also, don't reverse the order — the main noun is always on the RIGHT side of の.
PARTICLE の — ALL USE CASES
の = Noun Connector
① Possession ("'s")
わたしのほん
"my book"
② Affiliation ("of")
とうきょうだいがくのがくせい
"student of Tokyo Univ."
③ Description ("about")
にほんごのほん
"Japanese language book"
④ Chain multiple の
たなかさんのでんわばんごうのめーる
"Tanaka's phone number's email"
CRITICAL RULES
Modifier FIRST, Main LAST
[Noun1] の [Noun2]
Noun1 describes Noun2
❌ Only connects NOUNS
たべるのごはん ❌
Cannot use with verbs!
❌ Don't reverse order
ほんのわたし ❌
Main noun is ALWAYS right
GRAMMAR: THE PARTICLE も (MO)
KEY TERM: The "Me Too" Copycat (Similarity Marker)

Plain English: も (mo) means "also/too." It completely REPLACES は (wa) — they never appear together. Use it when two people/things share the same trait: "Tanaka is a student. Lee is also a student."

Affirmative Agreement:
A: わたしは さんねんせいです。 (I am a junior.)
B: ぼくも さんねんせいです。 (I am ALSO a junior.)

Negative Agreement ("neither/not either"):
A: モネさんは だいがくいんせい じゃありません。 (Monet is not a grad student.)
B: リーさんも だいがくいんせい じゃありません。 (Li is NOT a grad student EITHER.)

Common mistake: わたしはも がくせいです → わたしも がくせいです
🧠 Memory trick: も = "MOre of the same." It sounds like "more" and means "same thing applies to me too." When も arrives, は leaves — think of it as a hostile takeover!
⚠️ TRAP: も and は cannot coexist. も completely replaces は. Also works in negative sentences: わたしも がくせいじゃありません = I am also NOT a student.
PARTICLE も (MO) — "ALSO/TOO"
も REPLACES は (completely)
Affirmative
わたしは がくせいです。
リーさんも がくせいです。
"Lee is also a student."
Negative too!
わたしも がくせいじゃありません。
"I am also NOT a student."
❌ TRAPS
は and も NEVER coexist
わたしはも ❌ WRONG
わたしも ✅ RIGHT
も replaces を too
コーヒーも のみます。
"I also drink coffee."
NUMBERS, TIME & AGE
KEY TERM: The Base-10 Bullshit (Decimal System & Counters)

Basic Numbers 1-10:
1 いち, 2 に, 3 さん, 4 し/よん, 5 ご, 6 ろく, 7 しち/なな, 8 はち, 9 く/きゅう, 10 じゅう.

Telling Time (〜じ):
Pattern: [Number] + じ (ji) + です。
- AM: ごぜん (gozen). PM: ごご (gogo).
- Half past: はん (han). Ex: 4:30 = よじはん.

Irregular Times:
- 4:00 = よじ (yo-ji) [NOT yon-ji]
- 7:00 = しちじ (shichi-ji) [NOT nana-ji]
- 9:00 = くじ (ku-ji) [NOT kyuu-ji]
⚠️ TRAP: Saying "yon-ji" for 4 o'clock or "kyuu-ji" for 9 o'clock. 4, 7, and 9 are the cursed numbers in Japanese and almost always have irregular readings with counters.
TIME TELLING — IRREGULAR READINGS
⚠ IRREGULAR HOURS
4:00 = よじ (NOT よんじ)
7:00 = しちじ (NOT ななじ)
9:00 = くじ (NOT きゅうじ)
✅ REGULAR HOURS
1:00 いちじ   2:00 にじ   3:00 さんじ
5:00 ごじ   6:00 ろくじ   8:00 はちじ
10:00 じゅうじ   11:00 じゅういちじ
12:00 じゅうにじ   なんじ=What time?
Memory: 4, 7, 9 are the "cursed numbers" — they ALWAYS have irregular readings with counters.
JAPANESE CULTURE: BOWING & INTRODUCTIONS
KEY TERM: Titles & Meishi (Social Protocol)

1. Names & Titles:
- NEVER use your own title or 〜さん (san) for yourself.
- Use last names + さん for others unless they are a teacher/doctor, then use せんせい (sensee). First names are strictly for close friends/family.

2. Bowing (Ojigi):
- Replaces shaking hands. Bow with feet together, bend ~15 degrees from the waist, drop eyes.
- Deeper bow = more respect/apology.

3. Business Cards (名刺 meishi):
- Exchanged standing up, using BOTH hands.
- Read carefully to identify rank/affiliation before putting it away. Saying よろしく おねがいいたします is standard.
⚠️ TRAP: Introducing yourself as "Watashi wa Smith-san desu." Adding "-san" to your own name is extremely arrogant. Just say "Smith desu."
SELF-INTRODUCTION & NAME RULES
✅ CORRECT
About yourself (no さん!)
スミスです。
"I'm Smith."
About a teacher
たなかせんせい
"Prof. Tanaka" (use title!)
Business card (meishi)
Exchange with BOTH hands,
read carefully, say:
よろしくおねがいいたします
❌ WRONG
さん on yourself = arrogant
スミスさんです ❌
Never add さん to your own name!
さん for a teacher
たなかさん ❌
Teachers are せんせい, never さん!
TRAP SUMMARY: AVOID THESE COMMON MISTAKES
  1. Particle Pronunciation: は is written 'ha' but pronounced 'wa'. を is written 'wo' but pronounced 'o'.
  2. Question Word Placement: Do not move question words to the front. せんこうは なんですか (Major wa what desu ka), NOT なんですか せんこうは.
  3. Self-Titling: Never use 〜さん or せんせい for yourself.
  4. Negative with も: Do not drop the noun! わたしも がくせい じゃありません, NOT わたしも じゃありません.
  5. Double Consonants: っ is a full beat of silence (glottal stop). きって (ki-t-te).
  6. Noun modifier order: [Modifier] の [Main Noun]. "Japanese book" = にほんご の ほん.
  7. Irregular Time: 4:00 is よじ, 7:00 is しちじ, 9:00 is くじ.
  8. Greetings spelling: こんにちは and こんばんは end with は, NOT わ.

〜は にほんごで なんと いいますか~wa nihongo de nan to iimasu kaHow do you say ~ in Japanese?Ch 1 Expression

ちょっかくchokkakuright angleCh 1 Reading
しゃっくりshakkurihiccupCh 1 Reading

ぎゅうにゅうgyuunyuumilkCh 1 Reading

いまimanowCh 2 Noun

かんこくkankokuSouth KoreaCh 2 Noun
けいえいがくkeieigakumanagement, business adminCh 2 Noun
こうがくkoogakuengineeringCh 2 Noun
ごごgogop.m., afternoonCh 2 Noun
ごぜんgozena.m., morningCh 2 Noun
こちらkochirathis person, this wayCh 2 Noun
さんねんせいsannenseijunior, third-year studentCh 2 Noun
スペインSupeinSpainCh 2 Noun
せんこうsenkoomajorCh 2 Noun
だいがくdaigakucollege, universityCh 2 Noun
だいがくいんせいdaigakuinseegraduate studentCh 2 Noun
だいがくせいdaigakuseicollege studentCh 2 Noun
たいわんTaiwanTaiwanCh 2 Noun

なまえnamaenameCh 2 Noun

フランスFuransuFranceCh 2 Noun
ぶんがくbungakuliteratureCh 2 Noun
メキシコMekishikoMexicoCh 2 Noun

らいねんrainennext yearCh 2 Noun
りゅうがくせいryuugakuseiinternational studentCh 2 Noun
れきしrekishihistoryCh 2 Noun
ぼくbokuI (normally used by males)Ch 2 Pronoun
わたしwatashiI (used by both males and females)Ch 2 Pronoun
ですdesu(to) be (copula)Ch 2 Verb
いちじichijione o'clockCh 2 Time
にじnijitwo o'clockCh 2 Time
さんじsanjithree o'clockCh 2 Time
よじyojifour o'clockCh 2 Time
ごじgojifive o'clockCh 2 Time
ろくじrokujisix o'clockCh 2 Time
しちじshichijiseven o'clockCh 2 Time
はちじhachijieight o'clockCh 2 Time
くじkujinine o'clockCh 2 Time
じゅうじjuujiten o'clockCh 2 Time
じゅういちじjuuichijieleven o'clockCh 2 Time
じゅうにじjuunijitwelve o'clockCh 2 Time
はんhanhalf pastCh 2 Time
どこdokowhereCh 2 Question
どちらdochirawhere (polite), which wayCh 2 Question
なに / なんnani / nanwhatCh 2 Question
かkaquestion markerCh 2 Particle
のnonoun modifier marker (of, 's)Ch 2 Particle
はwatopic markerCh 2 Particle
もmosimilarity marker (also, too)Ch 2 Particle
お〜o~polite prefixCh 2 Prefix
〜ご~golanguage suffixCh 2 Suffix
〜じ~jio'clock suffixCh 2 Suffix
〜じん~jinnationality suffixCh 2 Suffix
〜せい~seistudent suffixCh 2 Suffix
〜ねん~nenyear suffixCh 2 Suffix
いいえ、そうじゃありません / そうじゃないですiie, soo ja arimasen / soo janai desuNo, that's not soCh 2 Expression
ええ / はい、そうですee / hai, soo desuYes, that's soCh 2 Expression
〜から きました~kara kimashitacame from ~Ch 2 Expression
こちらこそkochirakosoIt is I who should be saying that / Same here.Ch 2 Expression
そうですかsoo desu kaIs that so? I see.Ch 2 Expression
どうも ありがとう ございますdoomo arigatoo gozaimasuThank you very much.Ch 2 Expression

いがくigakumedicineCh 2 Supp

HIRAGANA あ～そ (ROWS あ・か・さ)
The first 15 hiragana — 3 fundamental rows:

RowCharactersRomajiNotes
あ行あ い う え おa i u e oBase vowels — foundation of ALL Japanese sounds
か行か き く け こka ki ku ke koHard k sound
さ行さ し す せ そsa shi su se so⚠️ し = shi (NOT si)

Useful expressions (forms of address):
• ～さん — Mr./Ms. (polite, NEVER for yourself)
• ～くん — for boys/male juniors
• ～ちゃん — for children/close friends
• せんせい — teacher (used as title, never for yourself)

Self-introduction pattern:
はじめまして。[name]です。どうぞよろしく。
HIRAGANA た～ほ (ROWS た・な・は)
RowCharactersRomajiNotes
た行た ち つ て とta chi tsu te to⚠️ ち = chi (NOT ti), つ = tsu (NOT tu)
な行な に ぬ ね のna ni nu ne noRegular
は行は ひ ふ へ ほha hi fu he ho⚠️ ふ = fu (NOT hu); は = wa as particle

Useful expressions (daily greetings):
• おはようございます — Good morning (formal)
• おはよう — Good morning (casual)
• こんにちは — Hello / Good afternoon
• こんばんは — Good evening
HIRAGANA ま～ん (ROWS ま・や・ら・わ・ん)
RowCharactersRomajiNotes
ま行ま み む め もma mi mu me moRegular
や行や ゆ よya yu yoOnly 3 characters
ら行ら り る れ ろra ri ru re ror = light tongue tap (between r and l)
わ行わ をwa woを = “o” sound, used only as particle
んんnOnly consonant that stands alone

Useful expressions (taking leave):
• さようなら — Goodbye (somewhat formal)
• じゃあ、また — See you later (casual)
• しつれいします — Excuse me (entering/leaving room)
• おやすみなさい — Good night
HIRAGANA LONG VOWELS (ああ～わあ)
Long vowel rules:

Vowel ColumnRuleExampleMeaning
あ column longadd あおかあさんmother
い column longadd いおにいさんolder brother
う column longadd うくうこうairport
え column longadd い (usually) ⚠️ NOT えせんせいteacher
お column longadd う (usually) ⚠️ NOT おがっこうschool

Useful expressions (understanding instructor):
• きいてください — Please listen
• みてください — Please look
• かいてください — Please write
• よんでください — Please read
• いってください — Please say it

1. What Is It

Long vowels stretch a vowel sound for exactly one extra beat (mora). Missing or adding a long vowel changes the meaning of a word entirely. The tricky part: the spelling doesn't always match the sound.

2. Rules Table — Long Vowel Spelling Rules

SoundSpelled WithExampleReadingException Spelled WithException Example
aaあおかあさんokaasan (mother)——
iiいおにいさんoniisan (older brother)——
uuうくうこうkuukou (airport)——
eeい (surprising!)せんせいsensee (teacher)え (rare)おねえさん (onesan, older sister)
ooう (surprising!)おとうさんotousan (father)お (rare)おおきい (ookii, big), とおい (tooi, far)

Exceptions List — Words That Use おお Instead of おう

WordReadingMeaning
おおきいookiibig
おおいooimany/much
とおいtooifar
とおりtooristreet
こおりkooriice
おおさかoosakaOsaka (city)

3. Contrast Examples

Short VowelMeaningLong VowelMeaning
おばさん (obasan)auntおばあさん (obaasan)grandmother
おじさん (ojisan)uncleおじいさん (ojiisan)grandfather
ここ (koko)hereこうこう (koukou)high school
え (e)pictureええ (ee)yes (casual)

4. Mnemonic

"E cheats with I, O cheats with U"
The two "cheater" vowels: long "e" is spelled with い and long "o" is spelled with う. They don't use their own letter — they cheat by borrowing from a neighbor.

The rare おお exceptions: "Big things are far away on an icy Osaka street"
おおきい (big), とおい (far), こおり (ice), おおさか (Osaka), とおり (street).

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP: Spelling long "o" with お by default
❌ WRONG: がっこお for school
✅ RIGHT: がっこう — use う for long "o" in 95% of cases
Only the ~6 exception words above use おお.

7. Cheat Sheet

Long SoundDefault SpellingRare SpellingMemory Aid
aa+ あ—Straightforward
ii+ い—Straightforward
uu+ う—Straightforward
ee+ い (cheater!)+ え (おねえさん)E cheats with I
oo+ う (cheater!)+ お (big/far/ice)O cheats with U
HIRAGANA SMALL っ (DOUBLE CONSONANTS)
Rule: Small っ doubles the NEXT consonant. Hold the consonant slightly longer.

With っRomajiWithout っRomajiMeaning difference
きってki-tteきてki-testamp vs. come
がっこうga-kkouがこう—school
いっぱいi-ppaiいぱい—full / a lot
ざっしza-sshiざし—magazine

Useful expressions (confirming/requesting):
• もういちどおねがいします — One more time please
• ちょっとまってください — Please wait a moment
• すみません、もういちど — Excuse me, once more

1. What Is It

The small っ (chiisai tsu) creates a double consonant by inserting a one-beat pause before the next consonant. It is NOT pronounced as "tsu" — it is a moment of held silence that makes the next consonant pop harder.

2. Rules Table

RuleDetail
SizeWritten SMALLER than regular つ. Compare: つ (big) vs っ (small)
PlacementAlways BEFORE the doubled consonant: きって
Only beforek, s, t, p consonants (never before vowels, n, m, r)
Mora countCounts as 1 full beat of silence
PronunciationStop airflow, hold tension, then release into the next consonant

3. Contrast Examples — Minimal Pairs

Without っBeatsMeaningWith っBeatsMeaning
きて (kite)2come!きって (kitte)3stamp / cut!
さか (saka)2hillさっか (sakka)3writer
かた (kata)2shoulderかった (katta)3won / bought
もと (moto)2originもっと (motto)3more
いた (ita)2was (animate)いった (itta)3said / went

4. Mnemonic

"The tiny っ is a speed bump"
Imagine driving: you hit a speed bump (っ) and your car pauses before continuing. The word きって = ki → [speed bump] → te.

Physical trick: Clap on each beat. For きって, clap 3 times: ki [silent clap] te.

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Pronouncing small っ as "tsu"
❌ WRONG: Saying きって as "ki-tsu-te"
✅ RIGHT: Say "kit-te" with a held pause before "te"
Small っ is SILENT — it just doubles the following consonant.
⚠️ TRAP #2: Writing big つ instead of small っ
❌ きつて — this reads as "ki-tsu-te" (nonsense)
✅ きって — small っ is visually smaller and sits lower
Size matters! Always check if your っ is small enough.

7. Cheat Sheet

FeatureDetail
Symbolっ (small tsu)
FunctionDoubles next consonant, adds 1 beat of pause
Valid beforek, s, t, p ONLY
PronunciationHold/stop air, then release into next consonant
DangerChanges word meaning completely
HIRAGANA GLIDES (きゃ～ぴょ)
Combination chart — small ゃ/ゅ/ょ after い-column kana:

Base+ゃ (ya)+ゅ (yu)+ょ (yo)
ききゃ kyaきゅ kyuきょ kyo
ししゃ shaしゅ shuしょ sho
ちちゃ chaちゅ chuちょ cho
ににゃ nyaにゅ nyuにょ nyo
ひひゃ hyaひゅ hyuひょ hyo
みみゃ myaみゅ myuみょ myo
りりゃ ryaりゅ ryuりょ ryo
ぎぎゃ gyaぎゅ gyuぎょ gyo
じじゃ jaじゅ juじょ jo
びびゃ byaびゅ byuびょ byo
ぴぴゃ pyaぴゅ pyuぴょ pyo
⚠️ The small kana (ゃゅょ) must be SMALL — large versions are different characters!

Useful expressions (asking for JP/EN):
• にほんごでなんですか — What is it in Japanese?
• えいごでなんですか — What is it in English?
• ～はにほんごでなんですか — What is ~ in Japanese?

1. What Is It

Glides (combination sounds) fuse an i-column character with a small ゃ, ゅ, or ょ into a single beat. The "i" vowel disappears and gets replaced by the ya/yu/yo sound. They are one of the most common sources of reading errors.

2. Rules Table

RuleDetail
Base characterMUST be from the i-column (き、し、ち、に、ひ、み、り、ぎ、じ、び、ぴ)
Added characterMUST be SMALL ゃ、ゅ、ょ
Beat countThe combination = 1 single mora (not two)
Sound changeDrop the "i" vowel, add ya/yu/yo: き(ki) + ゃ = kya (not ki-ya)
Irregular readingsしゃ=sha, しゅ=shu, しょ=sho / ちゃ=cha, ちゅ=chu, ちょ=cho / じゃ=ja, じゅ=ju, じょ=jo

3. Contrast Examples — Size Matters!

Small (Glide = 1 beat)ReadingBig (Separate = 2 beats)ReadingMeaning Difference
きゃkya (1 beat)きやki-ya (2 beats)Different sounds entirely
びょういんbyouin (hospital)びよういんbiyouin (beauty salon)Hospital vs hair salon!
じゅうjuu (ten)じゆうjiyuu (freedom)Number vs concept
しょうshou (small)しようshiyou (let's use)Adjective vs verb

4. Mnemonic

"Small = Swallowed"
The small ゃゅょ gets "swallowed" by the preceding character — they share one beat. The big version stands on its own and gets its own beat.

"Hospital vs Beauty Salon" test: If you mix up びょういん and びよういん, you might go to the wrong place. This is the #1 example Japanese teachers use to show why size matters.

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Writing full-size や/ゆ/よ instead of small
❌ WRONG: きよう (ki-you, today = different word)
✅ RIGHT: きょう (kyou, today)
Always double-check the size of your ゃゅょ!
⚠️ TRAP #2: Combining with non-i-column characters
❌ WRONG: かゃ, すゅ — these don't exist
✅ RIGHT: Only i-column characters combine: き、し、ち、に、ひ、み、り (+ voiced versions)

7. Cheat Sheet

FeatureDetail
What combinesi-column kana + small ゃ/ゅ/ょ
Result1 beat, "i" drops out
DangerBig vs small changes meaning
Irregular readingssha/shu/sho, cha/chu/cho, ja/ju/jo
Key testびょういん (hospital) vs びよういん (salon)
KATAKANA (カタカナ) — COMPLETE REFERENCE
When to use Katakana:
  • Foreign/borrowed words (loanwords from English, etc.)
  • Foreign names and places
  • Onomatopoeia (sound effects)
  • Scientific/technical terms
  • Emphasis (like italics in English)

Basic Katakana Chart (46 Characters)

aiueo
-ア aイ iウ uエ eオ o
kカ kaキ kiク kuケ keコ ko
sサ saシ shiス suセ seソ so
tタ taチ chiツ tsuテ teト to
nナ naニ niヌ nuネ neノ no
hハ haヒ hiフ fuヘ heホ ho
mマ maミ miム muメ meモ mo
yヤ ya—ユ yu—ヨ yo
rラ raリ riル ruレ reロ ro
wワ wa———ヲ wo
nン n————

Voiced Katakana (Dakuten ゛)

aiueo
gガ gaギ giグ guゲ geゴ go
zザ zaジ jiズ zuゼ zeゾ zo
dダ daヂ jiヅ zuデ deド do
bバ baビ biブ buベ beボ bo
pパ paピ piプ puペ peポ po

Combination Katakana (Glides)

yayuyo
kキャ kyaキュ kyuキョ kyo
sシャ shaシュ shuショ sho
tチャ chaチュ chuチョ cho
nニャ nyaニュ nyuニョ nyo
hヒャ hyaヒュ hyuヒョ hyo
mミャ myaミュ myuミョ myo
rリャ ryaリュ ryuリョ ryo
gギャ gyaギュ gyuギョ gyo
jジャ jaジュ juジョ jo
bビャ byaビュ byuビョ byo
pピャ pyaピュ pyuピョ pyo

Special Katakana Rules

1. Long vowels: Use ー (long dash) instead of repeating the vowel
    コーヒー (koohii) = coffee
    ケーキ (keeki) = cake

2. Small ッ (double consonant): Same as hiragana っ
    ベッド (beddo) = bed
    カップ (kappu) = cup

3. Extra sounds for foreign words:
    ティ (ti), ディ (di), ファ (fa), フィ (fi), フェ (fe), フォ (fo)
    ヴァ (va), ヴィ (vi), ヴ (vu), ヴェ (ve), ヴォ (vo)

Common Katakana Words (Loanwords)

KatakanaRomajiEnglish
テレビterebitelevision
コンピューターkonpyuutaacomputer
アパートapaatoapartment
レストランresutoranrestaurant
コンサートkonsaatoconcert
スポーツsupootsusports
テニスtenisutennis
ジョギングjogingujogging
アルバイトarubaitopart-time job
ゲームgeemugame
ハンバーガーhanbaagaahamburger
コーヒーkoohiicoffee
ビールbiirubeer
パンpanbread
バスbasubus
タクシーtakushiitaxi
ホテルhoteruhotel
デパートdepaatodepartment store
スーパーsuupaasupermarket
⚠️ TRAPS:
• ン (n) vs ソ (so) vs シ (shi) — look similar! ン has vertical stroke; ソ and シ differ in stroke direction
• ツ (tsu) vs シ (shi) — ツ strokes go DOWN-right; シ strokes go UP-right
• ウ (u) vs ワ (wa) — ウ has 3 strokes; ワ has 2 strokes (no top-right dot)
• ー (long vowel mark) is ONLY used in katakana, never hiragana

1. What Is It

Katakana is the angular, sharp-edged phonetic script used for foreign loanwords, foreign names, onomatopoeia, and emphasis. It has the same 46 base sounds as hiragana but with different shapes, plus a special long vowel dash (ー) that hiragana does not use.

2. Rules Table — Katakana-Specific Rules

RuleDetailExample
Long vowelsUse dash ー (not extra vowel char)コーヒー not コオヒイ
Double consonantsSmall ッ (same as hiragana っ)ベッド (beddo)
Extra soundsSmall vowel kana create non-native soundsファ (fa), ティ (ti), ディ (di)
Foreign R/LBoth map to R-row in katakanaLarry = ラリー, Rally = ラリー
"V" soundsUse ヴ (u + dakuten) or just バ行Violin = ヴァイオリン or バイオリン

3. Confusable Pairs Diagram

PairCharacter 1Character 2How to Tell Apart
シ vs ツシ (shi)ツ (tsu)シ: dots go LEFT-to-RIGHT, curve sweeps UP. ツ: dots go TOP-to-BOTTOM, curve sweeps DOWN.
ン vs ソン (n)ソ (so)ン: dot LOW-LEFT, stroke goes UP-right. ソ: dot HIGH-LEFT, stroke goes DOWN-right.
ウ vs ワウ (u)ワ (wa)ウ has 3 strokes (top dot). ワ has 2 strokes (no top dot).
ク vs タク (ku)タ (ta)タ has horizontal crossbar. ク does not.
ヌ vs スヌ (nu)ス (su)ヌ has a loop. ス has a straight cross.

4. Mnemonic

シ vs ツ: "She smiles sideways, TSUnami crashes down"
シ (shi) looks like a smiling face turned sideways — the dots are horizontal like eyes, curve is a smile going UP.
ツ (tsu) looks like rain falling — dots are vertical like raindrops, curve sweeps DOWN like a wave.

ン vs ソ: "N kicks UP, SO falls DOWN"
ン (n) — the stroke kicks upward (think "N = uNder to up").
ソ (so) — the stroke falls downward (think "SO = SOmething falling down").

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Using ー (long vowel dash) in hiragana
❌ WRONG: せんせー
✅ RIGHT: せんせい — hiragana uses vowel characters for long vowels, not dashes
⚠️ TRAP #2: Mixing up シ/ツ and ン/ソ
シツ and ンソ are the #1 katakana mistake for beginners.
✅ TIP: Focus on stroke DIRECTION. Horizontal dots = シ. Vertical dots = ツ.

7. Cheat Sheet

FeatureKatakana RuleHiragana Rule
Long vowelsー dashAdd vowel character
Double consonantsSmall ッSmall っ (same)
GlidesSmall ャュョSmall ゃゅょ (same)
Visual styleAngular, sharpCurvy, rounded
Used forForeign, emphasis, soundsGrammar, native words
第三章 — DAILY ROUTINES
Ch 3: Daily Routines · 90 vocabulary items
CHAPTER 3 VOCABULARY — DAILY ROUTINES & TIME
JapaneseRomajiTypeEnglishNotes
あさasaNounmorningCh3
あさごはんasagohanNounbreakfastCh3
あさってasatteNounthe day after tomorrowCh3; relative time; no に
あしたashitaNountomorrowCh3; relative time; no に
うちuchiNounhomeCh3
えいがeigaNounmovieCh3
おとといototoiNounthe day before yesterdayCh3; relative time
おふろofuroNounbathCh3; おふろに はいります = take a bath
がっこうgakkouNounschoolCh3
かようびkayoubiNounTuesdayCh3; day of week; takes に
きのうkinouNounyesterdayCh3; relative time; no に
きょうkyouNountodayCh3; relative time; no に
きんようびkinyoubiNounFridayCh3
クラスkurasuNounclassCh3; loanword
げつようびgetsuyoubiNounMondayCh3
コーヒーkoohiiNouncoffeeCh3; loanword
ごはんgohanNounmeal, cooked riceCh3
こんしゅうkonshuuNounthis weekCh3; relative time; no に
こんばんkonbanNountonightCh3; relative time; no に
シャワーshawaaNounshowerCh3; loanword
しゅうまつshuumatsuNounweekendCh3
じゅぎょうjugyouNounclass, courseCh3
しゅくだいshukudaiNounhomeworkCh3
すいようびsuiyoubiNounWednesdayCh3
せいかつseikatsuNounlife, livingCh3
せんしゅうsenshuuNounlast weekCh3; relative time; no に
つぎtsugiNounnextCh3
テレビterebiNountelevision, TVCh3; loanword
でんわばんごうdenwa bangouNountelephone numberCh3
としょかんtoshokanNounlibraryCh3
どようびdoyoubiNounSaturdayCh3
にちようびnichiyoubiNounSundayCh3
ばんbanNounnight, eveningCh3
ばんごはんbangohanNounsupper, dinnerCh3
ひるhiruNounafternoonCh3
ひるごはんhirugohanNounlunchCh3
べんきょうbenkyouNounstudyCh3
ほんhonNounbookCh3
まいあさmaiasaNounevery morningCh3; no に
まいしゅうmaishuuNounevery weekCh3; no に
まいにちmainichiNounevery dayCh3; no に
まいばんmaibanNounevery nightCh3; no に
もくようびmokuyoubiNounThursdayCh3
ありますarimasuう-verb (Group 1)(to) exist (inanimate); (to) haveCh3; dict: ある; ⚠️ only for inanimate things
いきますikimasuう-verb (Group 1)(to) goCh3; dict: いく; ⚠️ て-form: いって (irregular)
かえりますkaerimasuう-verb (Group 1)(to) return, (to) go homeCh3; dict: かえる
のみますnomimasuう-verb (Group 1)(to) drinkCh3; dict: のむ
はいりますhairimasuう-verb (Group 1)(to) enter, (to) take (a bath)Ch3; dict: はいる
よみますyomimasuう-verb (Group 1)(to) readCh3; dict: よむ
あびますabimasuる-verb (Group 2)(to) take (a shower)Ch3; dict: あびる; シャワーを あびます
おきますokimasuる-verb (Group 2)(to) get up, (to) wake upCh3; dict: おきる
たべますtabemasuる-verb (Group 2)(to) eatCh3; dict: たべる
ねますnemasuる-verb (Group 2)(to) go to bed, (to) sleepCh3; dict: ねる
みますmimasuる-verb (Group 2)(to) see, (to) watchCh3; dict: みる
きますkimasuIrregular verb(to) comeCh3; dict: くる; ⚠️ irregular conjugation
しますshimasuIrregular verb(to) doCh3; dict: する; ⚠️ irregular conjugation
べんきょうしますbenkyou shimasuIrregular verb(to) studyCh3; dict: べんきょうする; する compound
いつitsuQuestion wordwhenCh3
ゼロ / れいzero / reiNounzeroCh3; number
いちichiNounoneCh3
にniNountwoCh3
さんsanNounthreeCh3
よん / しyon / shiNounfourCh3; ⚠️ two readings; よん more common
ごgoNounfiveCh3
ろくrokuNounsixCh3
なな / しちnana / shichiNounsevenCh3; ⚠️ two readings
はちhachiNouneightCh3
きゅう / くkyuu / kuNounnineCh3; ⚠️ two readings
じゅうjuuNountenCh3
~ふん~funCounter~ minute(s)Ch3; ⚠️ sound changes: いっぷん, さんぷん, よんぷん, ろっぷん, はっぷん, じゅっぷん
あまりamariAdverbnot very oftenCh3; ⚠️ always with negative verbs
いつもitsumoAdverbalwaysCh3
ぜんぜんzenzenAdverbnot at allCh3; ⚠️ always with negative verbs
たいていtaiteiAdverbusuallyCh3
ときどきtokidokiAdverbsometimesCh3
よくyokuAdverboften, wellCh3
でdeParticleat, in (location of action)Ch3; marks where action occurs
にniParticleat, on, in (point in time)Ch3; marks specific time
にniParticleto (goal, destination)Ch3; marks destination
へeParticleto (direction)Ch3; ⚠️ written へ pronounced え
をoParticledirect object markerCh3; ⚠️ written を pronounced お
こん~kon~Prefixthis ~Ch3; e.g. こんしゅう
まい~mai~Prefixevery ~Ch3; e.g. まいにち
~ごろ~goroSuffixabout ~ (time only)Ch3; ⚠️ only for time points; use ぐらい for duration
~ようび~youbiSuffixday of the weekCh3
CH3: RELATIVE TIME & DAYS OF THE WEEK
KEY TERM: time_is_a_bitch (relative_time_expressions)

Relative time words change depending on when you speak (e.g., today, tomorrow). Absolute time words are fixed (e.g., 3:00, Monday).

Days of the Week (〜ようび)
DayJapaneseRomaji
Sunにちようびnichiyoubi
Monげつようびgetsuyoubi
Tueかようびkayoubi
Wedすいようびsuiyoubi
Thuもくようびmokuyoubi
Friきんようびkin'youbi
Satどようびdoyoubi
⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT use the particle に with relative time words like きょう (today) or あした (tomorrow). Only use に with absolute times like 8:00 or days of the week.
RELATIVE TIME — COMPLETE TIMELINE
Day before yest.
おととい
Yesterday
きのう
TODAY
きょう
Tomorrow
あした
Day after tom.
あさって
Last week
せんしゅう
This week
こんしゅう
Next week
らいしゅう
Rule: Relative time words (きょう, あした, etc.) do NOT take the particle に. Only absolute times (はちじ, げつようび) take に.
CH3: VERB CLASSES & CONJUGATION
KEY TERM: verb_bullshit (verb_conjugation_groups)

Japanese verbs are split into three classes. The polite present form always ends in 〜ます (affirmative) or 〜ません (negative).

1. う-verbs (U-verbs)
いきます (go), かえります (return), のみます (drink), よみます (read), はいります (enter/bath).

2. る-verbs (Ru-verbs)
たべます (eat), みます (watch), おきます (wake up), ねます (sleep), あびます (shower).

3. Irregular Verbs
きます (come), します (do), べんきょうします (study).
⚠️ TRAP: かえります (return) and はいります (enter) look like they could be Ru-verbs, but they are U-VERBS. Memorize these exceptions!
VERB GROUP IDENTIFICATION & POLITE CONJUGATION
What group is this verb?
↓
Group 1: う-Verbs
Dictionary → ます
いく → いきます
のむ → のみます
かえる → かえります
❌ Negative
いきません
⚠ Impostor る-verbs!
かえります、はいります
しります、きります
Look like Group 2 but are Group 1!
Group 2: る-Verbs
Drop る, add ます
たべる → たべます
みる → みます
おきる → おきます
ねる → ねます
❌ Negative
たべません
ID rule: ends in -iru/-eru
(but check impostor list!)
Group 3: Irregular
する → します
べんきょうします
(study)
くる → きます
きます
(come)
Only 2 verbs!
Memorize: する & くる
All X+します compounds count

1. What Is It

Japanese verbs belong to one of three groups that determine how they conjugate. In polite speech (Ch 3 scope), you use the 〜ます form. Knowing a verb's group is critical because it affects every single conjugation you'll learn from here on (te-form, past tense, potential, etc.).

2. Rules Table — Verb Group Identification

GroupAlso CalledDictionary Form EndingHow to Identify (ます form)Ch3 Examples
Group 1う-verbs / GodanConsonant + uBefore ます: -i ending from i/chi/ri/mi/ni/ki/gi/shi/biいきます、のみます、よみます、かえります
Group 2る-verbs / Ichidan-eru or -iruBefore ます: -e or consonant drops cleanlyたべます、みます、おきます、ねます
Group 3Irregularする、くるMemorize: only 2 verbs (+ する compounds)きます、します、べんきょうします

Polite Conjugation Table

FormAffirmativeNegativeExample (のむ)
Non-past (present/future)〜ます〜ませんのみます / のみません
Past〜ました〜ませんでしたのみました / のみませんでした

3. Contrast Examples

SentenceMeaningVerb Group
まいにち コーヒーを のみます。I drink coffee every day.Group 1 (u-verb)
あさごはんを たべます。I eat breakfast.Group 2 (ru-verb)
にほんごを べんきょうします。I study Japanese.Group 3 (irregular)

4. Mnemonic

"The Impostor List" — U-verbs disguised as Ru-verbs
These look like they end in -iru/-eru but are actually Group 1:
かえります (return), はいります (enter), しります (know), きります (cut), はしります (run)
Memory trick: "Kaeru Hairu Shiru Kiru Hashiru" — "The returning entering knowing cutting runner" is always a U-verb.

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Assuming all -iru/-eru verbs are Ru-verbs
❌ WRONG: Treating かえる (return) as Group 2
✅ RIGHT: かえる is Group 1 — must memorize the exceptions
⚠️ TRAP #2: Using wrong particle with verbs of motion
❌ WRONG: がっこうをいきます
✅ RIGHT: がっこうにいきます — use に for destination with motion verbs
⚠️ TRAP #3: Forgetting でした in negative past
❌ WRONG: たべません (this is present negative, not past)
✅ RIGHT: たべませんでした (past negative = ません + でした)

6. Decision Tree — "What Group Is This Verb?"

Is it する or くる (or a する compound)?
YES ↓
Group 3
Irregular
NO ↓
Does dictionary form end in -iru or -eru?
NO ↓
Group 1
U-verb (100%)
YES ↓
Is it on the exception list?
YES
Group 1 (U-verb!)
NO
Group 2 (Ru-verb)

7. Cheat Sheet

GroupAffirmativeNegativePast Aff.Past Neg.
All groups (polite)〜ます〜ません〜ました〜ませんでした
CH3: PARTICLES (を, に, へ, で)
KEY TERM: particle_hell (case_marking_particles)

Particles mark the grammatical function of the noun preceding them.

を (o) - Direct Object
Marks what the action is done TO.
ほんを よみます。 (I read a book.)

に (ni) / へ (e) - Destination
Marks the goal of movement verbs.
がっこうに いきます。 (I go to school.)

で (de) - Location of Action
Marks WHERE an action happens.
としょかんで べんきょうします。 (I study AT the library.)

DEEP DIVE: に vs で

Functionにで
LocationWhere something EXISTS (static)Where an ACTION happens
DestinationWhere you're GOING TO—
TimeSpecific time point—
Means—By means of (tool/transport)
Scope—Within a range/context
Contrastive Examples:
に (existence/destination/time)で (action location/means)
としょかんに ほんが あります。
There ARE books in the library. (existence)		 としょかんで べんきょうします。
I study AT the library. (action)
うちに かえります。
I go home. (destination)		 うちで たべます。
I eat at home. (action location)
しちじに おきます。
I wake up at 7. (time point)		 バスで いきます。
I go by bus. (means)
にほんに すんでいます。
I live in Japan. (existence)		 にほんで はたらいています。
I work in Japan. (action)
いすに すわります。
I sit on the chair. (destination of body)		 にほんごで はなします。
I speak in Japanese. (means)
Quick Rule: Is the thing just BEING there? → に. Is something being DONE there? → で.
⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT confuse に and で. Use に for destination/existence (arriving AT, being AT). Use で for action location/means (doing something IN/AT a place, by means of). としょかんにべんきょうします ❌ is wrong!
ALL PARTICLES — COMPLETE REFERENCE
CORE PARTICLES
を Direct Object
コーヒーをのみます (drink coffee)
に Destination / Time / Existence
うちにかえります (go home)
くじにおきます (wake at 9)
で Location of action / Means
うちでたべます (eat at home)
バスでいきます (go by bus)
へ Direction (toward)
にほんへいきます (go to Japan)
と With / And (exhaustive)
ともだちといきます (go with a friend)
TOPIC & MODIFIER PARTICLES
は Topic ("as for...")
わたしはがくせいです
が Subject / New info
ねこがいます (there's a cat)
も Also / Too (replaces は/を)
リーさんもがくせいです
の Noun connector ("'s" / "of")
わたしのほん (my book)
か Question marker
がくせいですか。
から From  /  まで Until
くじからごじまで (9 to 5)
ね Right?  /  よ I tell you!
いいてんきですね。 (Nice weather, right?)
に vs で Quick Rule: Being there? → に  |  Doing something there? → で
CH3: FREQUENCY ADVERBS
KEY TERM: always_on_my_ass (frequency_adverbs)

Adverbs of frequency go BEFORE the verb. No particle is needed after them.

Positive Frequency (use with 〜ます)
いつも = always
たいてい = usually
よく = often
ときどき = sometimes

Negative Frequency (MUST use with 〜ません)
あまり = not very often
ぜんぜん = not at all / never
⚠️ TRAP: You MUST use negative verbs with あまり and ぜんぜん. Saying "ぜんぜん たべます" ❌ is grammatically impossible. It must be "ぜんぜん たべません" ✅.
FREQUENCY SPECTRUM — COMPLETE SCALE
100%50%0%
✅ With 〜ます (Affirmative)
いつも (100%) = always
いつもあさごはんをたべます。
たいてい (90%) = usually
たいていうちでたべます。
よく (50%) = often
よくてれびをみます。
ときどき (30%) = sometimes
ときどきえいがをみます。
❌ MUST use 〜ません (Negative)
あまり (20%) = not very often
あまりたべません。
あまりたべます ❌ WRONG
ぜんぜん (0%) = never / not at all
ぜんぜんたべません。
ぜんぜんたべます ❌ WRONG

1. What Is It

Frequency adverbs tell how often something happens. The critical rule in Japanese is that some adverbs require negative verb forms. Using あまり or ぜんぜん with an affirmative verb is a grammar error.

2. Rules Table — Complete Frequency Spectrum

FrequencyJapaneseEnglish%Verb Form RequiredPosition
Alwaysいつもalways100%Affirmative (〜ます)Before verb
Usuallyたいていusually~80%Affirmative (〜ます)Before verb
Oftenよくoften~60%Affirmative (〜ます)Before verb
Sometimesときどきsometimes~40%Affirmative (〜ます)Before verb or sentence start
Not oftenあまりnot very often~20%NEGATIVE only (〜ません)Before verb
Neverぜんぜんnot at all0%NEGATIVE only (〜ません)Before verb

3. Contrast Examples

CorrectMeaningIncorrectWhy Wrong
いつも コーヒーを のみます。I always drink coffee.——
あまり コーヒーを のみません。I don't drink coffee often.あまり コーヒーを のみます。あまり + affirmative = error
ぜんぜん べんきょうしません。I don't study at all.ぜんぜん べんきょうします。ぜんぜん + affirmative = error

4. Mnemonic

"Amari and Zenzen are NEGATIVE people"
Think of あまり and ぜんぜん as two grumpy, negative people who REFUSE to hang out with positive verbs. They will only work with 〜ません.

Order mnemonic: "I Take Your Time, And Zero"
Itsumo, Taitei, Yoku, Tokidoki, Amari, Zenzen = 100% down to 0%.

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ THE BIG TRAP: Using あまり/ぜんぜん with affirmative verbs
❌ WRONG: ぜんぜん たべます (I eat not at all??? Nonsense)
✅ RIGHT: ぜんぜん たべません (I don't eat at all)
Rule: あまり/ぜんぜん = negative adverb → MUST pair with negative verb.

7. Cheat Sheet

AdverbVerb FormMemory
いつも・たいてい・よく・ときどきAffirmative 〜ますHappy words, happy verbs
あまり・ぜんぜんNegative 〜ません ONLYGrumpy words, grumpy verbs
CH3: POLITE PAST TENSE
KEY TERM: history_repeats (past_tense_conjugation)

To form the polite past tense, change the suffix of the verb. This works identically for ALL verb classes (U, Ru, Irregular).

FormAffirmativeNegative
Present〜ます (-masu)〜ません (-masen)
Past〜ました (-mashita)〜ませんでした (-masen deshita)

Examples:
いきます → いきました (went)
たべません → たべませんでした (didn't eat)
⚠️ TRAP: Students often try to say "いきませんです" ❌. The correct past negative is ALWAYS 〜ませんでした ✅.
POLITE TENSE — ALL 4 FORMS
✅ AFFIRMATIVE
Present/Future: 〜ます
いきます
"I go / will go"
Past: 〜ました
いきました
"I went"
❌ NEGATIVE
Present/Future: 〜ません
いきません
"I don't go"
Past: 〜ませんでした
いきませんでした
"I didn't go"
Past negative is always 〜ませんでした. Never 〜ませんです.
第四章 — JAPANESE CITIES
Ch 4: Japanese Cities · 60 vocabulary items
CHAPTER 4 VOCABULARY — CITIES, PLACES & ADJECTIVES
JapaneseRomajiTypeEnglishNotes
アパートapaatoNounapartmentCh4; loanword
えきekiNounstationCh4
えんぴつenpitsuNounpencilCh4
かばんkabanNounluggage, bagCh4
カフェkafeNouncoffee shop (modern)Ch4; newer vs きっさてん
きっさてんkissatenNouncoffee shop (traditional)Ch4; traditional term
きょうかしょkyoukashoNountextbookCh4
ぎんこうginkouNounbankCh4
けしゴムkeshigomuNouneraserCh4
こうえんkouenNounparkCh4
こうばんkoubanNounpolice boxCh4
このへんkono henNounthis area, around hereCh4
コンビニkonbiniNounconvenience storeCh4; loanword
じしょjishoNoundictionaryCh4
スーパーsuupaaNounsupermarketCh4; loanword
たてものtatemonoNounbuilding, structureCh4
テストtesutoNountestCh4; loanword
デパートdepaatoNoundepartment storeCh4; loanword
ノートnootoNounnotebookCh4; loanword
ビルbiruNounbuilding (tall)Ch4; loanword
びょういんbyouinNounhospitalCh4
ペンpenNounpenCh4; loanword
ボールペンboorupenNounballpoint penCh4; loanword
ほんやhonyaNounbookstoreCh4
まちmachiNountownCh4
ゆうびんきょくyuubinkyokuNounpost officeCh4
りょうryouNoundormitoryCh4
レストランresutoranNounrestaurantCh4; loanword
いますimasuる-verb (Group 2)(to) be, (to) exist (animate)Ch4; dict: いる; ⚠️ only for people/animals
あそこasokoDemonstrativeover there (place)Ch4; far from both
あれareDemonstrativethat one over thereCh4; far from both
ここkokoDemonstrativehere (place)Ch4; near speaker
これkoreDemonstrativethis (thing)Ch4; near speaker
そこsokoDemonstrativethere (place)Ch4; near listener
それsoreDemonstrativethat (thing)Ch4; near listener
あおいaoiい-AdjectiveblueCh4; color
あかいakaiい-AdjectiveredCh4; color
あたらしいatarashiiい-AdjectivenewCh4
いいiiい-AdjectivegoodCh4; ⚠️ irregular: neg=よくない, past=よかった
おおきいookiiい-AdjectivebigCh4
きいろいkiiroiい-AdjectiveyellowCh4; color
くろいkuroiい-AdjectiveblackCh4; color
しろいshiroiい-AdjectivewhiteCh4; color
たかいtakaiい-Adjectivetall, highCh4; also "expensive"
ちいさいchiisaiい-AdjectivesmallCh4
ちゃいろいchairoiい-AdjectivebrownCh4; color
ふるいfuruiい-Adjectiveold (things)Ch4; ⚠️ only for objects, NOT people
きれい (な)kirei (na)な-Adjectiveclean, pretty, neatCh4; ⚠️ TRAP: looks い-adj but is な-adj!
ゆうめい (な)yuumei (na)な-AdjectivefamousCh4
りっぱ (な)rippa (na)な-Adjectivefine, splendid, niceCh4
だれdareQuestion wordwhoCh4
どれdoreQuestion wordwhich oneCh4; choice among 3+ items
どんなdonnaQuestion wordwhat kind ofCh4; followed by noun
どうもdoumoAdverbvery; thanks (abbrev.)Ch4; short for どうもありがとう
とてもtotemoAdverbveryCh4; ⚠️ only affirmative; use あまり with negative
~や~yaSuffix~ storeCh4; e.g. ほんや
CH4: EXISTENCE (あります VS います)
KEY TERM: dead_or_alive_shit (existence_verbs)

To say "there is" or "I have", Japanese splits the universe into two categories: things that move on their own, and things that don't.

あります (Arimasu)
Use for INANIMATE objects: buildings, books, plants, events.
あそこに ぎんこうが あります。 (There is a bank over there.)

います (Imasu)
Use for ANIMATE beings: people, animals, pets.
としょかんに やまださんが います。 (Yamada is in the library.)

DEEP DIVE: Edge Cases

The core rule is "can it move under its own power?" but several categories are tricky:
ThingVerbWhy
Plants / TreesありますAlive, but cannot move on their own
Robots / Carsあります (usually)Machines, not living beings — unless personified
Events / MeetingsありますAbstract occurrences: かいぎが あります "There is a meeting"
Fish in a tankいますLiving creature that moves: さかなが います
Fish at the store (food)ありますProduct/dead: さかなが あります "They have fish (for sale)"
Dolls / Stuffed animalsありますObjects, not alive
Bugs / InsectsいますLiving creatures that move
Taxi (with driver)いますTreating it as containing a person: タクシーが います
⚠️ TRAP: Trees and plants are alive, but they don't move on their own. Therefore, use あります for trees/plants! Also, buildings use あります, never います.
⚠️ TRAP: さかな can be either! Swimming in a pond = います. Sitting on a plate = あります. Context is everything.
あります vs います — COMPLETE DECISION TREE
Does it move on its own?
↓
あります (Inanimate)
Objects: ほんがあります
"There is a book."
Buildings: えきがあります
"There is a station."
Plants/Trees: きがあります
"There is a tree."
Events: パーティーがあります
"There is a party."
Fish as food: さかながあります
"There is fish (on plate)."
います (Animate)
People: せんせいがいます
"There is a teacher."
Animals: ねこがいます
"There is a cat."
Fish (alive): さかながいます
"There is a fish (swimming)."
Robots/vehicles with people:
タクシーがいます
"There's a taxi (with driver)."
Pattern: [Place] に [Thing] が あります/います  |  Plants are alive but use あります (they don't move!)
CH4: DEMONSTRATIVES (KO-SO-A-DO)
KEY TERM: pointing_fingers (demonstrative_pronouns)

The Ko-So-A-Do system dictates distance relative to the speaker and listener.

Things (Standalone Nouns)
これ = This (near me)
それ = That (near you)
あれ = That over there (far from both)
どれ = Which one?

Places
ここ = Here
そこ = There
あそこ = Over there
どこ = Where?
⚠️ TRAP: これ, それ, あれ stand ALONE. Do NOT attach them directly to a noun. "これほん" ❌ is wrong. It must be "これは ほんです" ✅.
こそあど SYSTEM — COMPLETE GRID
こ〜
Near speaker		 そ〜
Near listener		 あ〜
Far from both		 ど〜
Question
Thing
(standalone)		 これ
this one		 それ
that one		 あれ
that one (far)		 どれ
which one?
Thing+Noun
(modifier)		 このN そのN あのN どのN
Place ここ
here		 そこ
there		 あそこ
over there		 どこ
where?
Direction こちら
this way		 そちら
that way		 あちら
that way (far)		 どちら
which way?
これ/それ/あれ stand ALONE. この/その/あの MUST attach to a noun. "これほん" ❌ "このほん" ✅

1. What Is It

The Ko-So-A-Do system is how Japanese points at things. Every demonstrative word starts with one of four prefixes based on distance from the speaker and listener. This is a grid system — once you learn the pattern, you can predict dozens of words.

2. Full Ko-So-A-Do Grid

Categoryこ- (Near Speaker)そ- (Near Listener)あ- (Far from Both)ど- (Question)
Thing (pronoun)これ thisそれ thatあれ that over thereどれ which one?
Thing + Nounこの＋N this Nその＋N that Nあの＋N that N over thereどの＋N which N?
Placeここ hereそこ thereあそこ over thereどこ where?
Directionこちら this wayそちら that wayあちら that way (far)どちら which way?

3. Contrast Examples — これ vs この

CorrectTranslationIncorrectWhy Wrong
これは ほんです。This is a book.これほんは...これ stands alone, cannot attach to noun
このほんは おもしろいです。This book is interesting.これのほんは...この (not これの) modifies nouns
どれが いいですか。Which one is good?どれの ほんが...Use どの before nouns

4. Mnemonic

"Ko = Close, So = So-so (middle), A = Away, Do = Don't know"
こ = Close to me (ko- sounds like "close")
そ = So-so distance (near you, medium)
あ = Away from us both (far)
ど = Don't know (question)

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Using これ to modify a noun
❌ WRONG: これほん or これのほん
✅ RIGHT: このほん — use この/その/あの/どの BEFORE nouns
⚠️ TRAP #2: Confusing distance in conversation
If BOTH you and the listener can see a nearby object, but it's closer to the listener, use それ (not これ).
The system is based on proximity to speaker vs listener, not absolute distance.

7. Cheat Sheet

Need to...UsePattern
Point at a thing (no noun)これ/それ/あれ/どれStands alone: これは〜です
Modify a nounこの/その/あの/どの + NAlways followed by noun: このほん
Point at a placeここ/そこ/あそこ/どこStands alone: ここは〜です
CH4: ADJECTIVES (I-ADJ VS NA-ADJ)
KEY TERM: sneaky_bastards (na_adjectives_ending_in_i)

Japanese has two types of adjectives: い-adjectives and な-adjectives.

Modifying a Noun:
い-adj: Keep the い. (おおきい うち = big house)
な-adj: Add な. (きれいな うち = pretty house)

Predicate (End of Sentence):
い-adj: Add です. (うちは おおきいです。)
な-adj: Add です. (うちは きれいです。)

Negative Predicate:
い-adj: Drop い, add くありません.
(おおきくありません)
な-adj: Add じゃありません.
(きれいじゃありません)
⚠️ TRAP: The "Kiyuri" trap! きれい (pretty), ゆうめい (famous), and りっぱ (splendid) look like い-adjectives, but they are NA-ADJECTIVES. You must say きれいな, not きれいい.
い-adj vs な-adj — IDENTIFICATION & CONJUGATION
Does it end in い?
↓
い-Adjectives
Before noun: adj + noun
おおきいほん (big book)
✅ Affirm: adj + です
おおきいです
❌ Neg: drop い, add くありません
おおきくありません
Past: drop い, add かったです
おおきかったです
⚠ いい is IRREGULAR
いいです → よくありません
いいです → よかったです
な-Adjectives
Before noun: adj + な + noun
きれいなほん (pretty book)
✅ Affirm: adj + です
きれいです
❌ Neg: adj + じゃありません
きれいじゃありません
Past: adj + でした
きれいでした
⚠ FAKE い-adjectives!
きれい、ゆうめい、きらい
End in い but are な-adjectives!
きれいな ✅   きれいい ❌

1. What Is It

Japanese has two completely different kinds of adjectives that conjugate differently. い-adjectives change their ending to conjugate; な-adjectives behave more like nouns and use じゃありません for negation. The trap is that some な-adjectives look like い-adjectives.

2. Rules Table — Adjective Conjugation (Non-Past)

Functionい-adjectiveな-adjective
Before a nounおおきい + Noun (keep い)きれいな + Noun (add な)
Predicate (aff.)おおきいですきれいです
Predicate (neg.)おおきくありません (drop い)きれいじゃありません

The いい (good) Irregular

FormRegular い-adj patternいい (IRREGULAR)
Affirmativeおおきいですいいです (stays as いい)
Negativeおおきくありませんよくありません (changes to よく!)
Before nounおおきい + Nいい + N

3. Contrast Examples

い-adjectiveな-adjective (looks like い!)
おおきいうち (big house — keep い)きれいなうち (pretty house — add な)
おおきくありません (not big)きれいじゃありません (not pretty)

4. Mnemonic

"KiYuRi" — the Fake-い Club
Kirei (きれい pretty), Yuumei (ゆうめい famous), Rippa (りっぱ splendid).
These END in い but are NA-adjectives. Remember "KiYuRi" = impostors!

"いい is WEIRD": いい is the only adjective that changes its stem when conjugated. Affirmative = いい. Everything else = よ-.

5. TRAP Warnings

⚠️ TRAP #1: Treating きれい as an い-adjective
❌ WRONG: きれいくありません
✅ RIGHT: きれいじゃありません
きれい is a な-adjective despite ending in い!
⚠️ TRAP #2: Negating いい as いくありません
❌ WRONG: いくありません
✅ RIGHT: よくありません
いい changes to よ- in all forms except basic affirmative.

6. Decision Tree — "い-adj or な-adj?"

Does the word end in い?
NO ↓
な-adjective
(e.g., しずか、げんき)
YES ↓
Is it きれい/ゆうめい/りっぱ?
(KiYuRi exceptions)
YES
な-adjective!
NO
い-adjective

7. Cheat Sheet

Taskい-adjな-adjいい (irregular)
+ Noun〜い + N〜な + Nいい + N
Predicate〜い です〜 ですいいです
Negative〜くありません〜じゃありませんよくありません
CH4: SENTENCE FINAL PARTICLES (ね & よ)
KEY TERM: vibe_checkers (sentence_final_particles)

These particles go at the very end of a sentence to add nuance.

ね (ne) - Agreement / Confirmation
Used when you think the listener agrees with you, or you are asking for confirmation. Like saying "isn't it?" or "right?".
さむいですね。 (It's cold, isn't it?)

よ (yo) - Assertion / New Info
Used when you are telling the listener something they don't know. Like saying "you know" or "I tell you".
あそこに ぎんこうが ありますよ。 (There's a bank over there, just so you know.)
⚠️ TRAP: Don't use よ if the listener already knows the information (it sounds bossy). Don't use ね if giving totally new facts.
ね vs よ — SENTENCE-ENDING PARTICLES
ね (Ne) = Shared feeling
Both know → seeking agreement
いいてんきですね。
"Nice weather, right?"
たかいですね。
"It's expensive, isn't it?"
When to use:
Listener already knows/can see the info. You're confirming shared experience.
よ (Yo) = New info / assertion
Listener doesn't know → telling them
あそこにぎんこうがありますよ。
"There's a bank over there, FYI."
このえいが、おもしろいですよ。
"This movie is interesting, I'm telling you."
When to use:
Listener doesn't know yet. You're informing or emphasizing.
ね with new info = weird  |  よ with shared info = bossy. Match particle to context!
CH4: LOCATION + SUBJECT ([place]に [noun]が あります/います)
Pattern: [place]に [noun]が あります/います
“There is [X] in [place]”

Question form: [place]に なにが ありますか
“What is in [place]?”

Examples:
• つくえのうえにほんがあります。 — There is a book on the desk.
• へやにねこがいます。 — There is a cat in the room.
• こうえんになにがありますか。 — What is in the park?

⚠️ TRAP: は = asking WHERE something is / が = asking WHAT exists somewhere
• ほんはどこにありますか。 — Where is the book? (topic = book)
• つくえのうえになにがありますか。 — What is on the desk? (unknown = what)
CH4: KANJI INTRODUCTION
KEY TERM: character_building (ideograms)

First 5 Kanji introduced in Chapter 4.

KanjiMeaningKun (JP)On (CN)
大Bigおお(きい)ダイ / タイ
学Studyまな(ぶ)ガク
校School-コウ
先Beforeさきセン
生Life/Bornい(きる)セイ / ショウ

Key Compounds:
大学 (大だい学がく) = University
先生 (先せん生せい) = Teacher
学校 (学がっ校こう) = School
⚠️ TRAP: Note the small っ in 学校 (がっこう). It is NOT がくこう.
KANJI COMPOUND LOGIC — HOW MEANING BUILDS
大学
だいがく
University
大 (big) + 学 (study)
= "big study" = University
先生
せんせい
Teacher
先 (before) + 生 (life)
= "born before" = Teacher
学校
がっこう
School
学 (study) + 校 (building)
= "study building" = School
Note: がっこう (small っ!)
山 = やま (mountain)
川 = かわ (river)
Pictographs: look like what they mean!
On'yomi vs Kun'yomi
山: さん (on) / やま (kun)
Compounds usually use on'yomi.
Standalone usually uses kun'yomi.
COMPLETE TERM INDEX (CH 3 & 4)
KEY TERM: the_whole_enchilada (comprehensive_vocabulary_list)
Every single vocabulary word, grammar point, and proper noun from Chapters 3 & 4.

第五章 — JAPANESE HOMES
Ch 5: Japanese Homes · 78 vocabulary items

JapaneseRomajiTypeEnglishNotes
うえueNounon, above, overCh5; location noun
うしろushiroNounbehind, in back ofCh5; location noun

あがってくださいagatte kudasaiExpressionPlease come inCh5; inviting into home
