メールmeerue-mailCh 6
休みやすみ / yasumirest, absence, day offCh 6
休みの日やすみのひ / yasumi no hia day off, holidayCh 6
両親りょうしん / ryoushinparentsCh 6
料理りょうり / ryouricooking, cuisineCh 6
会いますあいます / aimasuto meet (う-verb)Ch 6
遊びますあそびます / asobimasuto play, have fun (う-verb)Ch 6
歩きますあるきます / arukimasuto walk (う-verb)Ch 6
言いますいいます / iimasuto say (う-verb)Ch 6
泳ぎますおよぎます / oyogimasuto swim (う-verb)Ch 6
書きますかきます / kakimasuto write (う-verb)Ch 6
聞きますききます / kikimasuto listen, ask (う-verb)Ch 6
話しますはなします / hanashimasuto talk (う-verb)Ch 6
待ちますまちます / machimasuto wait (う-verb)Ch 6
呼びますよびます / yobimasuto call, invite (う-verb)Ch 6
かけますkakemasuto make (a phone call) (る-verb)Ch 6
出かけますでかけます / dekakemasuto go out (る-verb)Ch 6
忙しいいそがしい / isogashiibusy (い-adj)Ch 6
嬉しいうれしい / ureshiihappy (い-adj)Ch 6
面白いおもしろい / omoshiroiinteresting (い-adj)Ch 6
悲しいかなしい / kanashiisad (い-adj)Ch 6
寂しいさびしい / sabishiilonely (い-adj)Ch 6
楽しいたのしい / tanoshiifun (い-adj)Ch 6
つまらないtsumaranaiboring (い-adj)Ch 6
難しいむずかしい / muzukashiidifficult (い-adj)Ch 6
易しい / 優しいやさしい / yasashiieasy / kind (い-adj)Ch 6
元気げんき / genkihealthy, cheerful (な-adj)Ch 6
残念ざんねん / zannensorry, regrettable (な-adj)Ch 6
大丈夫だいじょうぶ / daijoubuall right, no problem (な-adj)Ch 6
大変たいへん / taihentough, hard (な-adj)Ch 6
賑やかにぎやか / nigiyakalively, bustling (な-adj)Ch 6
暇ひま / himafree, idle (な-adj)Ch 6
どうdouhow?Ch 6
一緒にいっしょに / issho nitogetherCh 6
是非ぜひ / zehiby all means, I'd love toCh 6
ゆっくりyukkurislowly, relaxCh 6
とtowith / and (particle)Ch 6
にnito (recipient) / purpose (particle)Ch 6
そしてsoshiteand (conjunction)Ch 6
都合が悪くてつごうがわるくて / tsugou ga warukuteinconvenient (declining invite)Ch 6
用事があってようじがあって / youji ga attehave errands to attend toCh 6
ませんかmasen kawon't you? (invitation)Ch 6
そうですかsou desu kaI see / Is that so?Ch 6
そうですねsou desu neThat's right / I agreeCh 6
今日きょう / kyoutoday (irregular)Kanji
明日あした / ashitatomorrow (irregular)Kanji
昨日きのう / kinouyesterday (irregular)Kanji
週末しゅうまつ / shuumatsuweekendKanji
数 — NUMBERS & TIME
Reference Material
- SECTION: TRAVEL & TIME COMPLETE REFERENCE -->
TRAVEL & TIME — COMPLETE REFERENCE (Ch 5)

Numbers 1-10

#JapaneseReading
1一いち (ichi)
2二に (ni)
3三さん (san)
4四し / よん (shi / yon)
5五ご (go)
6六ろく (roku)
7七しち / なな (shichi / nana)
8八はち (hachi)
9九く / きゅう (ku / kyuu)
10十じゅう (juu)

Tens (じゅう Multiples)

#JapaneseReading
10十じゅう
20二十にじゅう
30三十さんじゅう
40四十よんじゅう
50五十ごじゅう
60六十ろくじゅう
70七十ななじゅう
80八十はちじゅう
90九十きゅうじゅう

Hundreds (ひゃく)

#JapaneseReadingNotes
100百ひゃく
200二百にひゃく
300三百さんびゃくSound change!
400四百よんひゃく
500五百ごひゃく
600六百ろっぴゃくSound change!
700七百ななひゃく
800八百はっぴゃくSound change!
900九百きゅうひゃく

Thousands (せん)

#JapaneseReadingNotes
1,000千せん
2,000二千にせん
3,000三千さんぜんSound change!
4,000四千よんせん
5,000五千ごせん
6,000六千ろくせん
7,000七千ななせん
8,000八千はっせんSound change!
9,000九千きゅうせん
10,000一万いちまん

Hours (じかん) — Duration Counter

DurationJapaneseNotes
1 hourいちじかん
2 hoursにじかん
3 hoursさんじかん
4 hoursよじかんNOT よんじかん
5 hoursごじかん
6 hoursろくじかん
7 hoursしちじかんNOT ななじかん
8 hoursはちじかん
9 hoursくじかんNOT きゅうじかん
10 hoursじゅうじかん

Minutes (ふん / ぷん)

MinJapaneseMinJapanese
1いっぷん6ろっぷん
2にふん7ななふん
3さんぷん8はっぷん / はちふん
4よんぷん9きゅうふん
5ごふん10じゅっぷん / じっぷん

Hours + Minutes Combos

TimeJapanese
1:30いちじさんじゅっぷん / いちじはん
3:15さんじじゅうごふん
4:45よじよんじゅうごふん
7:10しちじじゅっぷん
9:20くじにじゅっぷん

Age (さい)

AgeJapaneseNotes
1いっさいSound change
2にさい
3さんさい
4よんさい
5ごさい
6ろくさい
7ななさい
8はっさいSound change
9きゅうさい
10じゅっさいSound change
20はたちCompletely irregular!

Approximation

ぐらい / くらい = about, approximately. Attach after the number+counter.
Example: さんじかんぐらい = about 3 hours
Example: にじゅっぷんぐらい = about 20 minutes

Travel Master Pattern

[Start] から [End] まで [Transport] で [Time] ぐらい かかります。

Example: うちからだいがくまでバスでにじゅっぷんぐらいかかります。
(From home to the university, by bus, it takes about 20 minutes.)
⚠️ TRAP: あるいて (on foot) NEVER takes the particle で. Say あるいてじゅっぷんぐらいかかります, NOT あるいてでじゅっぷん.

Travel Q&A

QuestionAnswer Pattern
[Place A] から [Place B] まで どのぐらい かかりますか。[Transport] で [Time] ぐらい かかります。
How long does it take from A to B?By [transport], it takes about [time].

Duration Q&A Template

Q PatternA Pattern
どのぐらい [verb] ますか。[Time] ぐらい [verb] ます。
How long do you [verb]?About [time] I [verb].
文 — PATTERNS
Reference Material
- SECTION: SENTENCE PATTERN TEMPLATES -->
SENTENCE PATTERN TEMPLATES — Ch 1-6 (DETAILED)

Ch 2 Grammar Patterns

G1: X は Y です (Identity)

FormPatternExample
Affirmative[Topic] は [Noun] です。わたしは がくせいです。 (I am a student.)
Negative[Topic] は [Noun] じゃありません。わたしは せんせいじゃありません。 (I am not a teacher.)

G2: Question か

TypePatternExample
Yes/No[Statement] + か。キムさんは かんこくじんですか。
Info Q[Topic] は [Q-word] ですか。せんこうは なんですか。

G3: Noun の Noun (Modification)

UsePatternExample
Possession[Owner] の [Thing]わたしの ほん (my book)
Affiliation[Group] の [Member]とうきょうだいがくの がくせい

G4: も (Also / Too)

TypePatternExample
Affirmative[Topic] も [Noun] です。リーさんも がくせいです。 (Li is also a student.)
Negative[Topic] も [Noun] じゃありません。リーさんも せんせいじゃありません。

G5: なんじですか (What time?)

PatternExample
いま なんじですか。いま ごぜん くじです。 (It is 9:00 AM now.)

Ch 3 Grammar Patterns

G1: Verb Conjugation (Polite Non-Past)

FormAffirmativeNegative
Present〜ます〜ません
Past〜ました〜ませんでした

G2: Particle を (Direct Object)

PatternExample
[Object] を [Verb]ます。ほんを よみます。 (I read a book.)

G3: Particle に / へ (Destination / Time)

UsePatternExample
Destination[Place] に/へ いきます。がっこうに いきます。 (I go to school.)
Time[Time] に [Verb]ます。はちじに おきます。 (I wake up at 8.)

G4: Particle で (Location of Action)

PatternExample
[Place] で [Verb]ます。としょかんで べんきょうします。 (I study at the library.)

G5: Frequency Adverbs

AdverbMeaningVerb Form
いつもalwaysAffirmative (〜ます)
たいていusuallyAffirmative (〜ます)
よくoftenAffirmative (〜ます)
ときどきsometimesAffirmative (〜ます)
あまりnot oftenNEGATIVE (〜ません)
ぜんぜんnot at allNEGATIVE (〜ません)

Ch 4 Grammar Patterns

G1: あります / います (Existence)

TypePatternExample
Inanimate[Place] に [Thing] が あります。としょかんに ほんが あります。
Animate[Place] に [Being] が います。こうえんに いぬが います。

G2: Demonstratives (Ko-So-A-Do)

Near SpeakerNear ListenerFarQuestion
これ (this)それ (that)あれ (that over there)どれ (which)
ここ (here)そこ (there)あそこ (over there)どこ (where)

G3: Adjectives

TypeBefore NounPredicate (+)Predicate (-)
い-adjおおきい ほんおおきいですおおきくありません
な-adjきれいな へやきれいですきれいじゃありません

G4: ね / よ (Sentence-Final Particles)

ParticleUseExample
ねSeeking agreement ("right?")さむいですね。 (It's cold, isn't it?)
よGiving new info ("I tell you")あそこに ぎんこうが ありますよ。

Ch 5 Grammar Patterns

G1: この / その / あの / どの + Noun

PatternExample
この [Noun] = This [noun]このほんは おもしろいです。
その [Noun] = That [noun]そのかばんは やまださんのです。
あの [Noun] = That [noun] (far)あのひとは せんせいです。
どの [Noun] = Which [noun]?どのほんが いいですか。

G2: Pronoun の (Substitution)

TypePatternExample
い-adj[い-adj] + のあかいの (the red one)
な-adj[な-adj] + な + のきれいなの (the pretty one)
Noun[Noun] + のやまださんの (Yamada's one)

G3: Travel Particles (から / まで / で)

PatternExample
[A] から [B] まで [Transport] で [Time] ぐらい かかります。うちからだいがくまでバスでにじゅっぷんぐらいかかります。

G4: は vs が

Use がUse は
Subject IS a Q-word: だれが きますか。Topic is known: やまださんは どこですか。
New discovery: あ、ねこが います！Q-word in comment: がくしょくは どこですか。

G5: Location Nouns

PatternExample
[Ref] の [Location] に あります/います。つくえのしたに ねこが います。 (The cat is under the desk.)

Ch 6 Grammar Patterns

G1: て-Form (Verb Sequencing)

TypeRuleExample
る-verbDrop ます, add てたべます → たべて
う-verb (し)→ してはなします → はなして
う-verb (き)→ いてききます → きいて
う-verb (ぎ)→ いでおよぎます → およいで
う-verb (い/ち/り)→ ってまちます → まって
う-verb (み/び/に)→ んでよみます → よんで
Irregularきます → きて, します → して
EXCEPTIONいきます → いって (NOT いきて)

G2: Adjective Past Tense

TypePast (+)Past (-)
い-adj〜かったです〜くなかったです
な-adj / Noun〜でした〜じゃなかったです
いい (irregular)よかったですよくなかったです

G3: Adjective て-Form (Connecting)

TypeRuleExample
い-adjい → くてひろくて (spacious and...)
いいいい → よくてよくて (good and...)
な-adjstem + でにぎやかで (lively and...)

G4: Particle と ("with" / "and")

UsePatternExample
"With"[Person] と [Verb]ともだちと べんきょうしました。
"And" (nouns)[Noun] と [Noun]そうじと せんたくを しました。

G5: に for Purpose + Invitations

PatternExample
[Place] に [Verb Stem] に いくたいいくかんに およぎに いきます。 (Go to the gym to swim.)
[Verb]ませんか (Invitation)いっしょに ピクニックに いきませんか。 (Won't you go on a picnic together?)
Accept: [Verb]ましょう。ええ、いきましょう。 (Yes, let's go.)
Decline (softly)すみません、ちょっと つごうが わるくて。
話 — CONVERSATIONS
Reference Material
CHAPTER 1 CONVERSATION — First Day of Class (30 exchanges)

Setting: Two students meeting for the first time in a Japanese class. The teacher (せんせい) also speaks.

SpeakerJapaneseRomajiEnglish
せんせいおはようございます。Ohayou gozaimasu.Good morning.
A+Bおはようございます。Ohayou gozaimasu.Good morning.
せんせいきいてください。Kiite kudasai.Please listen.
A+Bはい。Hai.Yes.
せんせいわたしは たなかです。せんせいです。Watashi wa Tanaka desu. Sensei desu.I'm Tanaka. I'm the teacher.
せんせいはじめまして。どうぞよろしく。Hajimemashite. Douzo yoroshiku.How do you do. Nice to meet you.
A+Bはじめまして。どうぞよろしく。Hajimemashite. Douzo yoroshiku.How do you do. Nice to meet you.
せんせいなまえを いってください。Namae wo itte kudasai.Please say your name.
Aスミスです。はじめまして。Sumisu desu. Hajimemashite.I'm Smith. How do you do.
Bリーです。はじめまして。どうぞよろしく。Rii desu. Hajimemashite. Douzo yoroshiku.I'm Lee. How do you do. Nice to meet you.
せんせいみてください。Mite kudasai.Please look.
せんせいかいてください。Kaite kudasai.Please write.
Aすみません、もういちどおねがいします。Sumimasen, mou ichido onegai shimasu.Excuse me, one more time please.
せんせいはい。よんでください。Hai. Yonde kudasai.Yes. Please read.
Aありがとうございます。Arigatou gozaimasu.Thank you.
せんせいいいえ。Iie.Not at all.
せんせいわかりましたか。Wakarimashita ka.Did you understand?
Aはい、わかりました。Hai, wakarimashita.Yes, I understood.
Bすみません、わかりません。Sumimasen, wakarimasen.Sorry, I don't understand.
せんせいもういちどおねがいします。きいてください。Mou ichido onegai shimasu. Kiite kudasai.One more time please. Please listen.
Bはい。あ、わかりました。ありがとうございます。Hai. A, wakarimashita. Arigatou gozaimasu.Yes. Ah, I understood. Thank you.
Aすみません、「book」は にほんごで なんですか。Sumimasen, “book” wa nihongo de nan desu ka.Excuse me, what is “book” in Japanese?
せんせい「ほん」です。“Hon” desu.It's “hon.”
A「ほん」ですか。ありがとうございます。“Hon” desu ka. Arigatou gozaimasu.“Hon”? Thank you.
Bすみません、「ともだち」は えいごで なんですか。Sumimasen, “tomodachi” wa eigo de nan desu ka.Excuse me, what is “tomodachi” in English?
せんせい「friend」です。“Friend” desu.It's “friend.”
Bありがとうございます。Arigatou gozaimasu.Thank you.
せんせいじゃあ、きょうは ここまでです。Jaa, kyou wa koko made desu.Well then, that's all for today.
Aしつれいします。じゃあ、また。Shitsurei shimasu. Jaa, mata.Excuse me (leaving). See you later.
Bおやすみなさい。さようなら。Oyasumi nasai. Sayounara.Good night. Goodbye.
⚠️ Grammar & Expressions demonstrated: All classroom commands (きいて/みて/かいて/よんで/いってください), self-introduction pattern (はじめまして〜です〜どうぞよろしく), greetings (おはようございます, さようなら, じゃあまた, おやすみなさい, しつれいします), thanking/apologizing (ありがとうございます, すみません, いいえ), confirming (わかりましたか/わかりました/わかりません, もういちどおねがいします), asking JP/EN equivalents (にほんごでなんですか, えいごでなんですか)
CHAPTER 2 CONVERSATION — College Orientation Mixer (30 exchanges)

Setting: Three students at a university orientation, introducing themselves and learning about each other.

SpeakerJapaneseRomajiEnglish
Aはじめまして。わたしは やまだです。どうぞよろしく。Hajimemashite. Watashi wa Yamada desu. Douzo yoroshiku.How do you do. I'm Yamada. Nice to meet you.
Bはじめまして。リーです。どうぞよろしく。Hajimemashite. Rii desu. Douzo yoroshiku.How do you do. I'm Lee. Nice to meet you.
Aリーさんは がくせいですか。Rii-san wa gakusei desu ka.Are you a student, Lee?
Bはい、そうです。だいがくの にねんせいです。Hai, sou desu. Daigaku no ninensei desu.Yes, that's right. I'm a second-year university student.
Aそうですか。せんこうは なんですか。Sou desu ka. Senkou wa nan desu ka.I see. What is your major?
Bせんこうは にほんごです。やまださんの せんこうは なんですか。Senkou wa nihongo desu. Yamada-san no senkou wa nan desu ka.My major is Japanese. What is your major, Yamada?
Aえいごです。わたしは さんねんせいです。Eigo desu. Watashi wa sannensei desu.It's English. I'm a third-year.
Bリーさんは にほんじんですか。Rii-san wa nihonjin desu ka.Are you Japanese, Lee?
Bいいえ、にほんじんじゃありません。ちゅうごくじんです。Iie, nihonjin ja arimasen. Chuugokujin desu.No, I'm not Japanese. I'm Chinese.
Aそうですか。やまださんは？Sou desu ka. Yamada-san wa?I see. And you, Yamada?
Aわたしは アメリカじんです。りゅうがくせいです。Watashi wa amerikajin desu. Ryuugakusei desu.I'm American. I'm an exchange student.
Cすみません。はじめまして。スミスです。Sumimasen. Hajimemashite. Sumisu desu.Excuse me. How do you do. I'm Smith.
Aはじめまして。やまだです。どうぞよろしく。Hajimemashite. Yamada desu. Douzo yoroshiku.How do you do. I'm Yamada. Nice to meet you.
Cスミスさんは がくせいですか。Sumisu-san wa gakusei desu ka.Are you a student, Smith?
Cはい、いちねんせいです。Hai, ichinensei desu.Yes, I'm a first-year.
Aせんこうは なんですか。Senkou wa nan desu ka.What's your major?
Cまだ わかりません。スミスさんは どこから きましたか。Mada wakarimasen. Sumisu-san wa doko kara kimashita ka.I don't know yet. Where did you come from, Smith?
Cカナダから きました。カナダじんです。Kanada kara kimashita. Kanadajin desu.I came from Canada. I'm Canadian.
Bわたしも りゅうがくせいです。Watashi mo ryuugakusei desu.I'm also an exchange student.
Aリーさんも りゅうがくせいですか。Rii-san mo ryuugakusei desu ka.You're also an exchange student, Lee?
Bはい、わたしも りゅうがくせいです。Hai, watashi mo ryuugakusei desu.Yes, I'm also an exchange student.
Cあのひとは だれですか。Ano hito wa dare desu ka.Who is that person?
Aすずきせんせいです。にほんごの せんせいです。Suzuki-sensei desu. Nihongo no sensei desu.That's Professor Suzuki. A Japanese language teacher.
Cすずきせんせいの きょうかしょは なんですか。Suzuki-sensei no kyoukasho wa nan desu ka.What is Professor Suzuki's textbook?
A「なかま」です。にほんごの きょうかしょです。“Nakama” desu. Nihongo no kyoukasho desu.It's “Nakama.” A Japanese language textbook.
Bこれは わたしの でんわばんごうです。Kore wa watashi no denwa bangou desu.This is my phone number.
Aありがとうございます。これは わたしの めいしです。Arigatou gozaimasu. Kore wa watashi no meishi desu.Thank you. This is my business card.
Cやまださんの ともだちは にほんじんですか。Yamada-san no tomodachi wa nihonjin desu ka.Is your friend Japanese, Yamada?
Aはい、そうです。よねんせいです。Hai, sou desu. Yonensei desu.Yes, that's right. A fourth-year.
Bわたしの ともだちも よねんせいです。だいがくいんせいじゃありません。Watashi no tomodachi mo yonensei desu. Daigakuinsei ja arimasen.My friend is also a fourth-year. Not a grad student.
⚠️ Grammar demonstrated: G1 (は〜です / じゃありません — identity & negation), G2 (ですか — yes/no questions, はい·そうです / いいえ answers), G3 (の — possession: わたしの, せんせいの, にほんごの), G4 (question words: なん, だれ, どこから, なんねんせい), G5 (も — わたしも りゅうがくせいです, replaces は). All Ch 2 vocab used: わたし, あなた, あのひと, なまえ, だいがく, こうこう, せんこう, 1-4ねんせい, だいがくいんせい, りゅうがくせい, nationalities (にほんじん, アメリカじん, ちゅうごくじん, カナダじん), ほん, ともだち, にほんご, えいご, きょうかしょ, めいし, でんわばんごう
CHAPTER 3 CONVERSATION — Comparing Daily Schedules (30 exchanges)

Setting: Two college students comparing their daily routines and talking about what they did last week.

SpeakerJapaneseRomajiEnglish
Aまいにち なんじに おきますか。Mainichi nanji ni okimasu ka.What time do you get up every day?
Bたいてい ろくじはんに おきます。やまださんは。Taitei rokujihan ni okimasu. Yamada-san wa.I usually get up at 6:30. How about you, Yamada?
Aわたしは しちじに おきます。あさごはんを たべますか。Watashi wa shichiji ni okimasu. Asagohan wo tabemasu ka.I get up at 7:00. Do you eat breakfast?
Bはい、いつも あさごはんを たべます。コーヒーも のみます。Hai, itsumo asagohan wo tabemasu. Koohii mo nomimasu.Yes, I always eat breakfast. I also drink coffee.
Aわたしは あまり あさごはんを たべません。Watashi wa amari asagohan wo tabemasen.I don't eat breakfast very often.
Bそうですか。じゅぎょうは なんじからですか。Sou desu ka. Jugyou wa nanji kara desu ka.I see. What time does your class start?
Aくじから じゅうにじまで じゅぎょうが あります。Kuji kara juuniji made jugyou ga arimasu.I have class from 9:00 to 12:00.
Bわたしも くじから じゅぎょうが あります。げつようびと すいようびと きんようびです。Watashi mo kuji kara jugyou ga arimasu. Getsuyoubi to suiyoubi to kinyoubi desu.I also have class from 9:00. Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.
Aかようびは じゅぎょうが ありますか。Kayoubi wa jugyou ga arimasu ka.Do you have class on Tuesday?
Bいいえ、かようびは じゅぎょうが ありません。Iie, kayoubi wa jugyou ga arimasen.No, I don't have class on Tuesday.
Aひるごはんは どこで たべますか。Hirugohan wa doko de tabemasu ka.Where do you eat lunch?
Bよく がっこうの しょくどうで たべます。ときどき うちに かえります。Yoku gakkou no shokudou de tabemasu. Tokidoki uchi ni kaerimasu.I often eat at the school cafeteria. Sometimes I go home.
Aごごは なにを しますか。Gogo wa nani wo shimasu ka.What do you do in the afternoon?
Bたいてい としょかんで べんきょうします。しゅくだいを します。Taitei toshokan de benkyou shimasu. Shukudai wo shimasu.I usually study at the library. I do homework.
Aとしょかんで ほんを よみますか。Toshokan de hon wo yomimasu ka.Do you read books at the library?
Bはい、よく にほんごの ほんを よみます。Hai, yoku nihongo no hon wo yomimasu.Yes, I often read Japanese books.
Aよる なにを しますか。Yoru nani wo shimasu ka.What do you do at night?
Bばんごはんを たべます。それから、テレビを みます。Bangohan wo tabemasu. Sorekara, terebi wo mimasu.I eat dinner. After that, I watch TV.
Aおんがくを ききますか。Ongaku wo kikimasu ka.Do you listen to music?
Bときどき ききます。しんぶんは ぜんぜん よみません。Tokidoki kikimasu. Shinbun wa zenzen yomimasen.I sometimes listen. I never read newspapers.
Aわたしも しんぶんは ぜんぜん よみません。ざっしは ときどき よみます。Watashi mo shinbun wa zenzen yomimasen. Zasshi wa tokidoki yomimasu.I never read newspapers either. I sometimes read magazines.
Bなんじに ねますか。Nanji ni nemasu ka.What time do you go to bed?
Aたいてい じゅういちじに ねます。Taitei juuichiji ni nemasu.I usually go to bed at 11:00.
Bせんしゅうの どようびに なにを しましたか。Senshuu no doyoubi ni nani wo shimashita ka.What did you do last Saturday?
Aともだちに あいました。いっしょに えいがを みました。Tomodachi ni aimashita. Issho ni eiga wo mimashita.I met a friend. We watched a movie together.
Bそうですか。いい えいがでしたか。Sou desu ka. Ii eiga deshita ka.I see. Was it a good movie?
Aはい、とても よかったです。にちようびは うちで てがみを かきました。Hai, totemo yokatta desu. Nichiyoubi wa uchi de tegami wo kakimashita.Yes, it was very good. On Sunday I wrote letters at home.
Bわたしは にちようびに かいものに いきました。デパートで ふくを かいました。Watashi wa nichiyoubi ni kaimono ni ikimashita. Depaato de fuku wo kaimashita.I went shopping on Sunday. I bought clothes at the department store.
Aきのうは なにを しましたか。Kinou wa nani wo shimashita ka.What did you do yesterday?
Bきのうは がっこうに いきませんでした。うちで べんきょうしました。Kinou wa gakkou ni ikimasendeshita. Uchi de benkyou shimashita.Yesterday I didn't go to school. I studied at home.
Aテストが ありますか。Tesuto ga arimasu ka.Is there a test?
Bはい、あした にほんごの テストが あります。Hai, ashita nihongo no tesuto ga arimasu.Yes, there's a Japanese test tomorrow.
Aがんばりましょう。じゃあ、また あした。Ganbarimashou. Jaa, mata ashita.Let's do our best. See you tomorrow then.
⚠️ Grammar demonstrated: G1 (verbs + を direct object, に destination, で location of action, へ direction — たべます, いきます, べんきょうします), G2 (が あります — じゅぎょうが あります, テストが あります), G3 (time に — くじに, どようびに, なんじに), G4 (frequency adverbs — いつも, たいてい, よく, ときどき, あまり〜ません, ぜんぜん〜ません), G5 (past tense — みました, かきました, いきました, かいました, いきませんでした). All Ch 3 vocab used: あさ, ひる, よる, あさごはん, ひるごはん, ばんごはん, じゅぎょう, しゅくだい, テスト, としょかん, がっこう, うち, all verbs (たべます, のみます, いきます, きます, かえります, よみます, かきます, ききます, みます, します, べんきょうします, おきます, ねます, あいます, かいます), time words (きょう, あした, きのう, せんしゅう, まいにち, days of week), それから
CHAPTER 4 CONVERSATION — Exploring a New City (30 exchanges)

Setting: A student has just arrived in a Japanese city and is asking a local resident for help finding places.

SpeakerJapaneseRomajiEnglish
Aすみません、このへんに ぎんこうが ありますか。Sumimasen, kono hen ni ginkou ga arimasu ka.Excuse me, is there a bank around here?
Bはい、ありますよ。あの たかい たてものです。Hai, arimasu yo. Ano takai tatemono desu.Yes, there is. It's that tall building over there.
Aあれですか。おおきい たてものですね。Are desu ka. Ookii tatemono desu ne.That one? It's a big building, isn't it.
Bええ、あたらしい ぎんこうですよ。Ee, atarashii ginkou desu yo.Yes, it's a new bank, you know.
Aゆうびんきょくも ありますか。Yuubinkyoku mo arimasu ka.Is there also a post office?
Bゆうびんきょくは えきの ちかくに あります。Yuubinkyoku wa eki no chikaku ni arimasu.The post office is near the station.
Aえきは どこに ありますか。Eki wa doko ni arimasu ka.Where is the station?
Bあそこです。ほんやの となりです。Asoko desu. Honya no tonari desu.Over there. It's next to the bookstore.
Aこの まちに びょういんは ありますか。Kono machi ni byouin wa arimasu ka.Is there a hospital in this town?
Bはい、おおきい びょういんが あります。えきの うしろに あります。Hai, ookii byouin ga arimasu. Eki no ushiro ni arimasu.Yes, there's a big hospital. It's behind the station.
Aこうばんは どこですか。Kouban wa doko desu ka.Where is the police box?
Bこうばんは あの こうえんの まえに あります。Kouban wa ano kouen no mae ni arimasu.The police box is in front of that park.
Aあの こうえんは きれいですね。Ano kouen wa kirei desu ne.That park is pretty, isn't it.
Bええ、とても きれいな こうえんですよ。しずかですよ。Ee, totemo kirei na kouen desu yo. Shizuka desu yo.Yes, it's a very pretty park. It's quiet, you know.
Aこの まちは にぎやかですか。Kono machi wa nigiyaka desu ka.Is this town lively?
Bはい、とても にぎやかです。ゆうめいな まちですよ。Hai, totemo nigiyaka desu. Yuumei na machi desu yo.Yes, it's very lively. It's a famous town, you know.
Aやすい レストランは ありますか。Yasui resutoran wa arimasu ka.Is there a cheap restaurant?
Bはい、えきの ちかくに やすい レストランが たくさん あります。Hai, eki no chikaku ni yasui resutoran ga takusan arimasu.Yes, there are a lot of cheap restaurants near the station.
Aあの たてものは なんですか。Ano tatemono wa nan desu ka.What is that building?
Bあれは デパートです。ふるい デパートですが、いい デパートですよ。Are wa depaato desu. Furui depaato desu ga, ii depaato desu yo.That's a department store. It's old, but it's a good department store.
Aスーパーは ここに ありますか。Suupaa wa koko ni arimasu ka.Is there a supermarket here?
Bスーパーは ここには ありません。そこに コンビニが あります。Suupaa wa koko ni wa arimasen. Soko ni konbini ga arimasu.There's no supermarket here. There's a convenience store there.
Aホテルは どこに ありますか。Hoteru wa doko ni arimasu ka.Where is a hotel?
Bホテルは やまの ちかくに あります。きっさてんの よこです。Hoteru wa yama no chikaku ni arimasu. Kissaten no yoko desu.The hotel is near the mountain. It's next to the coffee shop.
Aはなやは ありますか。Hanaya wa arimasu ka.Is there a flower shop?
Bはい、デパートの となりに ちいさい はなやが あります。Hai, depaato no tonari ni chiisai hanaya ga arimasu.Yes, there's a small flower shop next to the department store.
Aあの かわは きれいですか。Ano kawa wa kirei desu ka.Is that river pretty?
Bあまり きれいじゃありません。でも、やまは とても きれいですよ。Amari kirei ja arimasen. Demo, yama wa totemo kirei desu yo.It's not very pretty. But the mountain is very pretty.
Aせんせいは どこに いますか。Sensei wa doko ni imasu ka.Where is the teacher?
Bせんせいは としょかんに います。Sensei wa toshokan ni imasu.The teacher is in the library.
Aとしょかんに がくせいも いますか。Toshokan ni gakusei mo imasu ka.Are there also students in the library?
Bはい、がくせいが たくさん います。Hai, gakusei ga takusan imasu.Yes, there are a lot of students.
Aべんりな まちですね。Benri na machi desu ne.It's a convenient town, isn't it.
Bええ、そうですね。Ee, sou desu ne.Yes, it is.
⚠️ Grammar demonstrated: G1 (これ/それ/あれ/どれ — あれですか, あれは デパートです), G2 (〜は〜に あります/います — location with existence verbs, ⚠️ あります for inanimate, います for people), G3 (adjectives — い-adj: おおきい, たかい, あたらしい, ふるい, ちいさい, やすい, いい / な-adj: きれいな, しずか, にぎやか, ゆうめい, べんり + negative: きれいじゃありません), G4 (〜に〜が あります/います — describing what exists at a location), G5 (ね confirmation, よ new info). All Ch 4 vocab used: えき, ぎんこう, びょういん, ゆうびんきょく, こうばん, ほんや, はなや, きっさてん, スーパー, デパート, レストラン, ホテル, コンビニ, こうえん, たてもの, まち, やま, かわ, ここ/そこ/あそこ, all adjectives
CHAPTER 5 CONVERSATION — Visiting a Friend's Apartment (30 exchanges)

Setting: Student A visits Student B's apartment. They discuss the rooms, furniture, locations of things, and how far the apartment is from places.

SpeakerJapaneseRomajiEnglish
Aリーさんの アパートは どんな アパートですか。Rii-san no apaato wa donna apaato desu ka.What kind of apartment is yours, Lee?
Bちいさい アパートですが、あたらしいですよ。Chiisai apaato desu ga, atarashii desu yo.It's a small apartment, but it's new.
Aへやが いくつ ありますか。Heya ga ikutsu arimasu ka.How many rooms are there?
Bふたつ あります。この へやは わたしの へやです。Futatsu arimasu. Kono heya wa watashi no heya desu.There are two. This room is my room.
Aひろいですね。まども おおきいですね。Hiroi desu ne. Mado mo ookii desu ne.It's spacious, isn't it. The window is big too.
Bええ。あの まどから やまが みえます。Ee. Ano mado kara yama ga miemasu.Yes. You can see the mountain from that window.
Aだいどころは どこですか。Daidokoro wa doko desu ka.Where is the kitchen?
Bあの ドアの むこうです。だいどころは あまり ひろくないです。Ano doa no mukou desu. Daidokoro wa amari hiroku nai desu.It's beyond that door. The kitchen is not very spacious.
Aおてあらいは どこですか。Otearai wa doko desu ka.Where is the restroom?
Bだいどころの となりです。Daidokoro no tonari desu.It's next to the kitchen.
Aつくえの うえに なにが ありますか。Tsukue no ue ni nani ga arimasu ka.What is on top of the desk?
Bほんと きょうかしょが あります。Hon to kyoukasho ga arimasu.There are books and textbooks.
Aつくえの したには なにが ありますか。Tsukue no shita ni wa nani ga arimasu ka.What is under the desk?
Bかばんが あります。Kaban ga arimasu.There's a bag.
Aいすの よこに なにが ありますか。Isu no yoko ni nani ga arimasu ka.What is next to the chair?
Bほんだなが あります。ほんだなの なかに ざっしが たくさん あります。Hondana ga arimasu. Hondana no naka ni zasshi ga takusan arimasu.There's a bookshelf. Inside the bookshelf there are lots of magazines.
Aベッドの まえに なにが ありますか。Beddo no mae ni nani ga arimasu ka.What is in front of the bed?
Bテーブルが あります。テーブルの うえに テレビが あります。Teeburu ga arimasu. Teeburu no ue ni terebi ga arimasu.There's a table. On top of the table there's a TV.
Aねこは どこに いますか。Neko wa doko ni imasu ka.Where is the cat?
Bベッドの うしろに います。Beddo no ushiro ni imasu.It's behind the bed.
Aおしいれは どこですか。Oshiire wa doko desu ka.Where is the closet?
Bへやの ひだりに あります。ベッドの みぎに まども あります。Heya no hidari ni arimasu. Beddo no migi ni mado mo arimasu.It's on the left side of the room. There's also a window to the right of the bed.
Aにわは ありますか。Niwa wa arimasu ka.Is there a garden?
Bはい、アパートの そとに ちいさい にわが あります。Hai, apaato no soto ni chiisai niwa ga arimasu.Yes, there's a small garden outside the apartment.
Aいい アパートですね。このアパートから だいがくまで どのぐらい かかりますか。Ii apaato desu ne. Kono apaato kara daigaku made dono gurai kakarimasu ka.It's a nice apartment. About how long does it take from this apartment to the university?
Bバスで にじゅっぷんぐらい かかります。Basu de nijuppun gurai kakarimasu.It takes about 20 minutes by bus.
Aあるいて どのぐらいですか。Aruite dono gurai desu ka.How about on foot?
Bあるいて よんじゅっぷんぐらいです。Aruite yonjuppun gurai desu.About 40 minutes on foot.
Aえきから アパートまでは。Eki kara apaato made wa.From the station to the apartment?
Bえきから じゅっぷんぐらいです。ちかいですよ。Eki kara juppun gurai desu. Chikai desu yo.About 10 minutes from the station. It's close.
Aスーパーは この アパートの ちかくに ありますか。Suupaa wa kono apaato no chikaku ni arimasu ka.Is there a supermarket near this apartment?
Bはい、あります。でも、デパートは ここには ありません。えきの ちかくに あります。Hai, arimasu. Demo, depaato wa koko ni wa arimasen. Eki no chikaku ni arimasu.Yes, there is. But there's no department store here. There's one near the station.
Aこの アパートは やすいですか。Kono apaato wa yasui desu ka.Is this apartment cheap?
Bあまり やすくないです。でも、しずかで、べんりですよ。Amari yasuku nai desu. Demo, shizuka de, benri desu yo.It's not very cheap. But it's quiet and convenient.
Aいい アパートですね。わたしのは ふるくて、ちいさいです。Ii apaato desu ne. Watashi no wa furukute, chiisai desu.It's a nice apartment. Mine is old and small.
⚠️ Grammar demonstrated: G1 (この/その/あの/どの + noun — この へや, あの まど, どの + noun), G2 (location nouns — うえ, した, まえ, うしろ, みぎ, ひだり, なか, そと, となり, よこ, ちかく with [ref]の[location]に pattern), G3 (pronoun の — わたしの, わたしのは), G4 (から/まで/で/ぐらい — アパートから だいがくまで バスで にじゅっぷんぐらい かかります, ⚠️ あるいて not あるいてで), G5 (は vs が — へやは ひろいです vs ほんだなが あります, double particles: したには, ここには). All location nouns, all Ch 5 vocab used.
CHAPTER 6 CONVERSATION — Planning the Weekend (30 exchanges)

Setting: Two friends talk about their past weekend and make plans for the next one, using past adjectives, te-form, and invitations.

SpeakerJapaneseRomajiEnglish
Aしゅうまつは どうでしたか。Shuumatsu wa dou deshita ka.How was the weekend?
Bあまり よくなかったです。Amari yokunakatta desu.It wasn't very good.
Aそうですか。どようびに なにを しましたか。Sou desu ka. Doyoubi ni nani wo shimashita ka.I see. What did you do on Saturday?
Bあさ そうじを して、せんたくを しました。Asa souji wo shite, sentaku wo shimashita.In the morning I cleaned and did laundry.
Aたいへんでしたね。ごごは。Taihen deshita ne. Gogo wa.That must have been tough. How about the afternoon?
Bごご うちで しゅくだいを しました。むずかしかったです。Gogo uchi de shukudai wo shimashita. Muzukashikatta desu.In the afternoon I did homework at home. It was difficult.
Aにちようびは どうでしたか。Nichiyoubi wa dou deshita ka.How was Sunday?
Bにちようびは つまらなかったです。うちで しんぶんを よんで、テレビを みました。Nichiyoubi wa tsumaranakatta desu. Uchi de shinbun wo yonde, terebi wo mimashita.Sunday was boring. I read the newspaper and watched TV at home.
Aわたしの しゅうまつは たのしかったですよ。Watashi no shuumatsu wa tanoshikatta desu yo.My weekend was fun, you know.
Bなにを しましたか。Nani wo shimashita ka.What did you do?
Aどようびに ともだちと かいものに いきました。Doyoubi ni tomodachi to kaimono ni ikimashita.On Saturday I went shopping with a friend.
Bなにを かいましたか。Nani wo kaimashita ka.What did you buy?
Aあたらしい ふくを かいました。やすくて、きれいでした。Atarashii fuku wo kaimashita. Yasukute, kirei deshita.I bought new clothes. They were cheap and pretty.
Bいいですね。Ii desu ne.That's nice.
Aそれから、ともだちと レストランで ばんごはんを たべました。おいしかったです。Sorekara, tomodachi to resutoran de bangohan wo tabemashita. Oishikatta desu.Then I ate dinner at a restaurant with my friend. It was delicious.
Bにちようびは。Nichiyoubi wa.How about Sunday?
Aにちようびは うんどうを して、さんぽを しました。てんきが よくて、きもちよかったです。Nichiyoubi wa undou wo shite, sanpo wo shimashita. Tenki ga yokute, kimochi yokatta desu.On Sunday I exercised and took a walk. The weather was good and it felt nice.
Bよるは なにを しましたか。Yoru wa nani wo shimashita ka.What did you do in the evening?
Aともだちに てがみを かいて、おんがくを ききました。Tomodachi ni tegami wo kaite, ongaku wo kikimashita.I wrote a letter to a friend and listened to music.
Bいそがしかったですね。Isogashikatta desu ne.You were busy, weren't you.
Aええ、でも たのしかったです。ところで、こんどの しゅうまつ、いっしょに えいがを みませんか。Ee, demo tanoshikatta desu. Tokoro de, kondo no shuumatsu, issho ni eiga wo mimasen ka.Yes, but it was fun. By the way, won't you watch a movie together this weekend?
Bいいですね。なんの えいがですか。Ii desu ne. Nan no eiga desu ka.That sounds good. What movie?
Aあたらしい にほんの えいがです。ゆうめいで、たのしいですよ。Atarashii nihon no eiga desu. Yuumei de, tanoshii desu yo.A new Japanese movie. It's famous and fun.
Bいきましょう。どようびは どうですか。Ikimashou. Doyoubi wa dou desu ka.Let's go. How about Saturday?
Aどようびは ちょっと いそがしいです。Doyoubi wa chotto isogashii desu.Saturday is a little busy.
Bじゃあ、にちようびは。Jaa, nichiyoubi wa.Then how about Sunday?
Aにちようびは ひまです。いいですよ。Nichiyoubi wa hima desu. Ii desu yo.Sunday I'm free. Sounds good.
Bにちようびの ごご いちじに えきの まえで あいましょう。Nichiyoubi no gogo ichiji ni eki no mae de aimashou.Let's meet in front of the station at 1:00 on Sunday afternoon.
Aいいですね。えいがの あとで、いっしょに コンサートに いきませんか。Ii desu ne. Eiga no ato de, issho ni konsaato ni ikimasen ka.Sounds good. After the movie, won't you go to a concert together?
Bコンサートですか。たのしそうですね。いきましょう。Konsaato desu ka. Tanoshi sou desu ne. Ikimashou.A concert? That sounds fun. Let's go.
Aじゃあ、にちようびに。Jaa, nichiyoubi ni.See you Sunday then.
Bええ、たのしみですね。Ee, tanoshimi desu ne.Yes, I'm looking forward to it.
Aところで、アルバイトは どうですか。しごとは いそがしいですか。Tokoro de, arubaito wa dou desu ka. Shigoto wa isogashii desu ka.By the way, how's the part-time job? Is work busy?
Bあまり いそがしくないです。でも、しつもんが おおいです。Amari isogashiku nai desu. Demo, shitsumon ga ooi desu.It's not very busy. But there are a lot of questions.
Aバイトは たのしいですか。Baito wa tanoshii desu ka.Is the job fun?
Bええ、やさしくて、たのしいです。Ee, yasashikute, tanoshii desu.Yes, it's easy and fun.
⚠️ Grammar demonstrated: G1 (と — ともだちと いきました / に — ともだちに てがみを かきました, かいものに いきました purpose), G2 (past adjectives — たのしかったです, つまらなかったです, むずかしかったです, よくなかったです ⚠️いい irregular, きれいでした な-adj past, いそがしかったです), G3 (て-form verbs — そうじをして, せんたくをしました / てください implicit), G4 (て-form adjectives — やすくて きれい, ゆうめいで たのしい, やさしくて たのしい, よくて きもちよかった), G5 (ませんか invitation — えいがをみませんか, コンサートにいきませんか / ましょう — いきましょう, あいましょう / ちょっと… soft decline). All Ch 6 vocab: えいが, おんがく, かいもの, さんぽ, うんどう, しごと, てがみ, しんぶん, ざっし, せんたく, そうじ, アルバイト/バイト, コンサート, テニス, しゅうまつ, こんど, しつもん, all adjectives (たのしい, つまらない, むずかしい, やさしい, いそがしい, ひま)
構 — STRUCTURE
Reference Material
═══════════════ STRUCT CH1 ═══════════════ -->
CH 1: SELF-INTRODUCTION & CLASSROOM REQUESTS

Self-Introduction Formula (はじめまして Pattern)

What is it? This is the #1 most important phrase pattern in Japanese. When you meet someone for the very first time, you use this 3-part formula. It is a ritual — every Japanese person uses it, and they expect you to use it too. Think of it like a handshake, but with words.

Why: You will use this in your very first conversation with ANY Japanese person. Without it, you cannot introduce yourself.

Structure: はじめまして。[Name] です。どうぞよろしく。

SlotWhat Goes HereWhat CANNOT Go Here
[Name]Your own name (first or last)Someone else's name; ~さん (never use for yourself)

Example 1:
はじめまして。たなか です。どうぞよろしく。
Hajimemashite. Tanaka desu. Douzo yoroshiku.
How do you do? I am Tanaka. Pleased to meet you.

Example 2:
はじめまして。メアリー です。どうぞよろしく。
Hajimemashite. Mearii desu. Douzo yoroshiku.
How do you do? I am Mary. Pleased to meet you.

⚠️ TRAP: Never attach ~さん to your own name. わたしは たなかさんです is WRONG. Only use ~さん / ~せんせい for OTHER people.
🧠 Memory trick: The 3 parts spell "H-D-D": Hajimemashite (nice to meet you), Desu (I am [name]), Douzo yoroshiku (please be kind to me). H-D-D. Always in this order!

More examples:

はじめまして。ジョン です。どうぞよろしく。
Hajimemashite. Jon desu. Douzo yoroshiku.
How do you do? I am John. Pleased to meet you.

Common mistake: はじめまして。ジョンさん です。 → はじめまして。ジョン です。
(Never use さん for yourself — it is like calling yourself "Mr.")

Classroom Requests (てください)

What is it? These are phrases your teacher uses to give instructions. てください means "please do [action]." You do NOT need to understand how て-form works yet (that is Ch 6). Just memorize these as set phrases, like memorizing "gesundheit" without knowing German.

Why: You will hear these in every single class. If you don't recognize them, you won't know what your teacher is asking you to do.

Structure: [Action word] + てください = "Please do ~"

JapaneseRomajiMeaning
きいてくださいkiite kudasaiPlease listen
いってくださいitte kudasaiPlease say / Please go
みてくださいmite kudasaiPlease look
よんでくださいyonde kudasaiPlease read
かいてくださいkaite kudasaiPlease write

Example 1:
すみません、もういちど いってください。
Sumimasen, mou ichido itte kudasai.
Excuse me, please say it one more time.

Example 2:
ここに なまえを かいてください。
Koko ni namae wo kaite kudasai.
Please write your name here.

⚠️ TRAP: てください is the polite request form. The て-form itself is learned formally in Ch 6, but these Ch 1 classroom phrases are memorized as set expressions.
CH 2: ALL 5 GRAMMAR POINTS — COMPLETE BREAKDOWN

G1: X は Y です / じゃありません

What is it? This is the most basic sentence pattern in Japanese. It lets you say "X is Y." The word です (desu) is like the English verb "is/am/are." The particle は (pronounced "wa," NOT "ha") marks the topic — it tells the listener what you are talking about.

When to use it: Introducing yourself, identifying things, describing what something is.

When NOT to use it: This pattern only works with NOUNS. You cannot say "I am tall" using this pattern (adjective sentences use a different structure, covered in Ch 4).

Why: This is sentence pattern #1. Every conversation you ever have in Japanese starts here.

🧠 Memory trick: Think of は as a spotlight on a stage. It shines a light on the topic: "Ladies and gentlemen, NOW we are talking about [X]!" Then です finishes the sentence: "...and it IS [Y]." Spotlight → punchline.

Structure: [Topic] は [Noun] です

The particle は (pronounced "wa") marks the topic — "As for X, it is Y."

FormPatternUsage
Affirmative[Topic] は [Noun] です。X is Y
Negative[Topic] は [Noun] じゃありません。X is not Y
SlotCAN Go HereCANNOT Go Here
[Topic]Any noun or pronoun (わたし、たなかさん、これ)Verbs, adjectives alone
[Noun]Nouns only (がくせい、にほんじん、せんせい)Verbs, adjectives (use different patterns)

Example 1:
わたしは がくせいです。
Watashi wa gakusei desu.
I am a student.

Example 2:
キムさんは にほんじんじゃありません。
Kimu-san wa nihonjin ja arimasen.
Kim is not Japanese.

⚠️ TRAP: は is WRITTEN as は (ha) but PRONOUNCED "wa" when used as the topic particle. Do not confuse with the hiragana は in words like はし (chopsticks) where it IS pronounced "ha."

G2: ですか Questions

What is it? In English, you turn a statement into a question by changing word order ("You are a student" → "Are you a student?"). Japanese is WAY easier: just add か to the end. That's it. No word rearranging, no "do/does," nothing. The sentence stays exactly the same — you just stick か on the end like a question mark.

Why: You cannot have a conversation without asking questions. This is the simplest grammar rule in all of Japanese.

🧠 Memory trick: か = verbal question mark. In English, "?" goes at the end. In Japanese, か goes at the end. Same job, different costume.

Structure: [Statement] + か (rising intonation optional)

Question TypePatternAnswer Pattern
Yes/No[Noun] ですか。はい、[Noun] です。/ いいえ、[Noun] じゃありません。
Confirmation[Topic] は [Noun] ですか。はい、そうです。/ いいえ、ちがいます。

Example 1:
りゅうがくせいですか。 — はい、りゅうがくせいです。
Ryuugakusei desu ka. — Hai, ryuugakusei desu.
Are you an international student? — Yes, I am.

Example 2:
たなかさんは にねんせいですか。 — いいえ、いちねんせいです。
Tanaka-san wa ninensei desu ka. — Iie, ichinensei desu.
Is Tanaka a second-year? — No, a first-year.

⚠️ TRAP: Answer patterns — はい、そうです (Yes, that's right) vs いいえ、ちがいます (No, that's wrong). Do NOT say いいえ、そうじゃありません — use ちがいます for contradiction.

G3: の Particle

What is it? The particle の connects two nouns together. It is like the English "'s" (possessive) or "of." It says "Noun A belongs to / describes / is affiliated with Noun B." The thing AFTER の is the main noun; the thing BEFORE の tells you more about it.

Why: Without の, you cannot say "my book," "Japanese teacher," or "University of Tokyo student." It is in virtually every sentence.

🧠 Memory trick: Think of の as a "glue stick" between two nouns. It always sticks the descriptor (left) onto the main noun (right). わたし (me) + の (glue) + ほん (book) = my book. Read it right-to-left in English: "book OF me" = my book.

Structure: [Noun A] の [Noun B] — A modifies/describes B

UsePatternExample
Possession[Owner] の [Thing]わたしの でんわばんごう (my phone number)
Affiliation[Group] の [Member]とうきょうだいがくの がくせい (a Tokyo Univ. student)
Description[Descriptor] の [Noun]にほんごの せんせい (a Japanese language teacher)

Example 1:
メアリーさんは アリゾナだいがくの がくせいです。
Mearii-san wa Arizona daigaku no gakusei desu.
Mary is a student of Arizona University.

Example 2:
たけしさんの でんわばんごうは 186-7343 です。
Takeshi-san no denwa bangou wa 186-7343 desu.
Takeshi's phone number is 186-7343.

⚠️ TRAP: の links nouns ONLY. You cannot use の between a verb and a noun (e.g., NOT たべるの ごはん).

G4: Question Words

What is it? These are the Japanese versions of "who, what, where, when." You plug them into the same は〜ですか sentence pattern. The question word goes where the answer would go. So if the answer is がくせいです (I'm a student), the question is なんですか (What are you?).

Why: Without question words, you can only ask yes/no questions. These let you ask open-ended questions and actually learn things about people.

🧠 Memory trick: All question words start with な or ど: なに (what), だれ (who — from な-row), どこ (where), どれ (which). When you hear a word starting with ど, your brain should immediately think: "Question incoming!"

Structure: [Topic] は [Question Word] ですか

Question WordMeaningExample Question
なん / なにwhatせんこうは なんですか。 (What is your major?)
だれwhoあのひとは だれですか。 (Who is that person?)
どこwhereがっこうは どこですか。 (Where is the school?)
どこからfrom whereどこからですか。 (Where are you from?)
なんねんせいwhat year (student)なんねんせいですか。 (What year are you?)

Example 1:
せんこうは なんですか。 — えいごです。
Senkou wa nan desu ka. — Eigo desu.
What is your major? — English.

Example 2:
メアリーさんは どこからですか。 — アメリカからです。
Mearii-san wa doko kara desu ka. — Amerika kara desu.
Where is Mary from? — From America.

⚠️ TRAP: なん vs なに — Use なん before です, before counters (なんじ、なんねんせい), and before の. Use なに before particles like を.

G5: も Particle (Also/Too)

What is it? も means "also" or "too." It works just like は, but adds the meaning of "same as someone else." The critical rule: も REPLACES は. They never appear together. Think of も as は's jealous twin — it kicks は out and takes its spot.

Why: In any conversation where people share something in common ("I'm also a student," "I also like sushi"), you need も.

🧠 Memory trick: も = "Me too!" Notice they even sound similar: mo = me too. When you want to say "me too" or "X also," swap は for も.
🔄 Don't confuse: は vs も
• たけしさんは にほんじんです。 = Takeshi is Japanese. (introducing new info)
• みちこさんも にほんじんです。 = Michiko is also Japanese. (adding "same as Takeshi")
• みちこさんはも にほんじんです。 = WRONG! は and も cannot coexist.

Structure: [Topic] も [Noun] です — も REPLACES は

FormPatternMeaning
Affirmative[X] も [Noun] です。X is also [Noun].
Negative[X] も [Noun] じゃありません。X is also not [Noun].

Example 1:
たけしさんは にほんじんです。みちこさんも にほんじんです。
Takeshi-san wa nihonjin desu. Michiko-san mo nihonjin desu.
Takeshi is Japanese. Michiko is also Japanese.

Example 2:
メアリーさんは にねんせいじゃありません。ロバートさんも にねんせいじゃありません。
Mearii-san wa ninensei ja arimasen. Robaato-san mo ninensei ja arimasen.
Mary is not a second-year. Robert is also not a second-year.

⚠️ TRAP: も REPLACES は. Never write は も together. WRONG: たけしさんはも. RIGHT: たけしさんも. Also, も works in negative sentences — it means "also not."
CH 3: ALL 5 GRAMMAR POINTS — COMPLETE BREAKDOWN

G1: Verb Particles を / に・へ / で

What is it? Particles are tiny words that tell you the role of each noun in a sentence. English uses word ORDER to show roles ("Dog bites man" vs "Man bites dog"). Japanese uses PARTICLES instead, so word order is flexible. There are 3 essential verb particles:

  • を (wo/o) = marks what you DO the action TO (the "target"). "I eat sushi" → すしを たべます
  • に / へ (ni / e) = marks WHERE YOU GO (destination). "I go to school" → がっこうに いきます
  • で (de) = marks WHERE YOU DO something (location of action). "I study at the library" → としょかんで べんきょうします

Why: Without particles, your listener cannot tell what you are doing, where you are going, or what you are acting upon. They are the skeleton of every sentence.

🧠 Memory trick: Think of particles as "name tags" on nouns. を = "Hi, I'm the TARGET." に = "Hi, I'm the DESTINATION." で = "Hi, I'm the LOCATION." The verb at the end reads all the name tags and knows what's happening.
🔄 Don't confuse に vs で:
• がっこうに いきます = I go TO school (destination, motion verb)
• がっこうで べんきょうします = I study AT school (location of activity)
Ask yourself: "Am I moving toward it?" → に. "Am I doing something there?" → で.

Structure: [Noun] + [Particle] + [Verb]ます

ParticleFunctionSlot RuleExample
をDirect objectThing being acted uponコーヒーを のみます (drink coffee)
に / へDestination / goalPlace you GO TOがっこうに いきます (go to school)
でPlace of actionPlace where action HAPPENSとしょかんで べんきょうします (study at the library)

に vs で Trap Table

SituationCorrectWhy
Go TO schoolがっこうに いきますに = destination (motion verb)
Study AT the libraryとしょかんで べんきょうしますで = location of activity
Go homeうちに かえりますに = destination
Eat AT homeうちで たべますで = where eating happens

Example 1:
わたしは としょかんで にほんごを べんきょうします。
Watashi wa toshokan de nihongo wo benkyou shimasu.
I study Japanese at the library.

Example 2:
まいにち がっこうに いきます。
Mainichi gakkou ni ikimasu.
I go to school every day.

⚠️ TRAP: に = WHERE YOU GO (destination). で = WHERE YOU DO SOMETHING (location of action). がっこうに いきます (go to school) vs がっこうで べんきょうします (study at school). Using the wrong one is a common exam mistake!

G2: が あります (Existence — Inanimate)

What is it? This pattern says "there is [something]" or "I have [something]." It announces that something EXISTS. The particle が marks the thing that exists. Think of it as pointing at something and saying "That thing? It exists." Use this for events (tests, parties) and inanimate things (books, desks).

Why: You need this to talk about schedules ("There is a test tomorrow"), ownership ("I have a car"), and describing scenes ("There is a park nearby").

🧠 Memory trick: あります = "a-thing exists" (A for objects/things). います = "is alive" (I for living things). Inanimate = あります. Alive = います.

Structure: [Event/Thing] が あります

SlotCAN Go HereCANNOT Go Here
Before が ありますEvents (テスト、クラス), inanimate things, plantsPeople, animals (use います instead)

Example 1:
きょう テストが あります。
Kyou tesuto ga arimasu.
There is a test today.

Example 2:
にちようびに パーティーが あります。
Nichiyoubi ni paatii ga arimasu.
There is a party on Sunday.

⚠️ TRAP: Plants use あります (inanimate), NOT います. にわに きが あります (There is a tree in the garden) is CORRECT. Also, events (classes, tests, parties) always use あります.

G3: Time に — Required vs Optional vs Never

What is it? When you mention a time in a sentence, you sometimes need the particle に after it (like "at" or "on" in English). The tricky part: some time words need に, some optionally take it, and some NEVER take it. The rule of thumb: if the time is a specific number (9 o'clock, Sunday, June), use に. If it is relative (today, tomorrow, every day), skip it.

Why: Using に incorrectly with time words is one of the top 5 mistakes on beginner exams.

🧠 Memory trick: If you could put a number on it (9:00, Monday, March), it takes に. If it is a relative word that changes meaning depending on when you say it (today, tomorrow, every day), no に. "Today" is only "today" TODAY — relative = no に.

Structure: [Time Expression] (に) [Verb]

に Requiredに Optionalに NEVER
Specific numbers:
くじに (at 9:00)
にちようびに (on Sunday)
ろくがつに (in June)		Relative time:
あした(に)
きょう(に)
こんしゅう(に)		Frequency/vague:
まいにち (every day)
いつも (always)
いつ (when?)

から〜まで: [Time A] から [Time B] まで = from A to B

Example 1:
くじに おきます。
Kuji ni okimasu.
I wake up at 9:00.

Example 2:
げつようびから きんようびまで はたらきます。
Getsuyoubi kara kinyoubi made hatarakimasu.
I work from Monday to Friday.

⚠️ TRAP: まいにち (every day), まいしゅう (every week), いつも (always) NEVER take に. WRONG: まいにちに. RIGHT: まいにち.

G4: Frequency Adverbs

What is it? These words tell how OFTEN you do something: always, usually, sometimes, never. They go before the verb. The big trap: the last two (あまり and ぜんぜん) are negative-only words. They MUST be paired with a negative verb (ません). Using them with an affirmative verb is like saying "I never eat" with a positive verb — it makes no sense.

Why: Frequency words appear in nearly every daily conversation. Getting あまり/ぜんぜん wrong is a guaranteed point loss on exams.

🧠 Memory trick: Think of a traffic light. Green words (いつも、たいてい、よく、ときどき) go with green/affirmative verbs (ます). Red words (あまり、ぜんぜん) go with red/negative verbs (ません). Red + green = crash. Never mix them!

Structure: [Frequency word] + [Verb]

AdverbFrequencyRequired Verb Form
いつも100% alwaysAffirmative (ます)
たいてい~80% usuallyAffirmative (ます)
よく~60% oftenAffirmative (ます)
ときどき~40% sometimesAffirmative (ます)
あまり~20% not muchMUST use ません ⚠️
ぜんぜん0% neverMUST use ません ⚠️

Example 1:
わたしは よく としょかんに いきます。
Watashi wa yoku toshokan ni ikimasu.
I often go to the library.

Example 2:
ぜんぜん テレビを みません。
Zenzen terebi wo mimasen.
I don't watch TV at all.

⚠️ TRAP: あまり and ぜんぜん MUST be paired with negative verb (ません). WRONG: ぜんぜん いきます. RIGHT: ぜんぜん いきません. This is a guaranteed exam question.

G5: Past Tense ました / ませんでした

What is it? To talk about things that already happened, swap the verb ending. ます (present/future) becomes ました (past). ません (negative) becomes ませんでした (negative past). That is the entire rule. No irregular verbs, no exceptions — just change the ending.

Why: You cannot tell stories, describe your day, or answer "What did you do?" without past tense.

🧠 Memory trick: ました = "ma-SHI-ta" — think "sh*t, it already happened!" The past tense. For the negative past, just glue でした onto ません: ませんでした = "didn't do." Never rearrange the pieces.

Structure:

TenseAffirmativeNegative
Non-past~ます~ません
Past~ました~ませんでした

Example 1:
きのう にほんごを べんきょうしました。
Kinou nihongo wo benkyou shimashita.
I studied Japanese yesterday.

Example 2:
きのう テレビを みませんでした。
Kinou terebi wo mimasen deshita.
I did not watch TV yesterday.

⚠️ TRAP: The negative past is ませんでした (NOT ましたません or ませんました). It is ません + でした as one unit.
CH 4: ALL 5 GRAMMAR POINTS — COMPLETE BREAKDOWN

G1: これ / それ / あれ / どれ Demonstratives

What is it? These are the Japanese words for "this one / that one / that one over there / which one?" They point at things. Japanese has THREE levels of distance (English only has two: "this" and "that"). The extra level distinguishes "that thing near you" from "that thing way over there."

Why: Pointing at things and asking about them is how most real-world conversations start — shopping, asking directions, ordering food.

🧠 Memory trick: KO-SO-A-DO
• KOre = Close to me ("ko" = "close")
• SOre = So close to you ("so" = near the person you're talking to)
• Are = Away from both of us ("a" = "away")
• DOre = Don't know — which one? ("do" = "dunno")

Structure: [Demonstrative] は [Noun] です

WordDistanceMeaning
これNear speakerthis one
それNear listenerthat one (by you)
あれFar from boththat one (over there)
どれUnknownwhich one?

Example 1:
これは いくらですか。
Kore wa ikura desu ka.
How much is this?

Example 2:
あれは たなかさんの かばんです。
Are wa Tanaka-san no kaban desu.
That (over there) is Tanaka's bag.

⚠️ TRAP: これ vs この — これ stands ALONE as a pronoun (これは ほんです). この must be followed by a noun (このほんは...). WRONG: これほん. WRONG: この は.

G2: Location あります / います

What is it? This pattern says "There is [something] at [place]." Japanese splits "there is" into TWO verbs: あります for non-living things (books, buildings, events) and います for living things (people, animals). English doesn't make this distinction, so it feels strange at first, but it becomes natural quickly.

Why: You need this to describe any scene, give directions, or say what is located where.

🔄 Don't confuse あります vs います:
• ほんが あります = There is a book (object → あります)
• ねこが います = There is a cat (living → います)
• きが あります = There is a tree (plants = inanimate → あります)
• Fish swimming = います. Fish on a plate = あります. (Alive vs food)

Structure: [Place] に [Thing] が あります / います

Location WordDistance
ここhere (near speaker)
そこthere (near listener)
あそこover there (far from both)
VerbUsed ForExamples
ありますInanimate objects, plants, eventsbooks, trees, parties
いますAnimate beingspeople, animals, insects

Example 1:
あそこに ぎんこうが あります。
Asoko ni ginkou ga arimasu.
There is a bank over there.

Example 2:
にわに ねこが います。
Niwa ni neko ga imasu.
There is a cat in the garden.

⚠️ TRAP: Animate vs Inanimate — Fish = います (alive). Fish on a plate = あります (food/object). Plants/trees = あります (inanimate). Robots = あります (machine).

G3: Adjectives い vs な

What is it? Japanese has two types of adjectives, and they follow DIFFERENT rules. い-adjectives end in い and change their own ending to become negative. な-adjectives look more like nouns and use じゃありません for negation. When placing an adjective before a noun, い-adjectives just go directly (おおきい まち), but な-adjectives need な glued on (しずかな まち).

Why: You cannot describe anything without adjectives. Using the wrong conjugation pattern is the #1 adjective mistake.

🧠 Memory trick: い-adjectives are independent — they change THEMSELVES (drop い, add くない). な-adjectives are needy — they need helpers (じゃありません, でした) to conjugate, just like nouns do.
Functionい-adjectiveな-adjective
Modifier (before noun)おおきい まち (big town)しずかな まち (quiet town)
Predicate (sentence end)まちは おおきいですまちは しずかです
Negativeおおきくないですしずかじゃありません

Example 1:
とうきょうは おおきいです。
Toukyou wa ookii desu.
Tokyo is big.

Example 2:
このまちは しずかじゃありません。
Kono machi wa shizuka ja arimasen.
This town is not quiet.

⚠️ TRAP #1: いい (good) is IRREGULAR. Negative = よくないです (NOT いくないです). All conjugations use よ- stem except the plain affirmative.
⚠️ TRAP #2: きれい (pretty/clean) LOOKS like an い-adjective but is a な-adjective! Negative: きれいじゃありません (NOT きれくないです). Same for ゆうめい (famous).

G4: Scene Description [Place] に [Noun] が

What is it? This is about choosing between は and が in existence sentences. The difference: が introduces new information ("Hey, there's a cat over there!"). は discusses already-known information ("The cat? It's on the chair."). Think of が as the "news flash" particle and は as the "follow-up" particle.

Why: This は vs が distinction is tested heavily and is essential for natural-sounding Japanese.

🔄 Don't confuse は vs が in location sentences:
• Q: "What's on the desk?" → A: つくえのうえに ほんが あります (NEW info: a book!)
• Q: "Where is the book?" → A: ほんは つくえのうえに あります (The book is ALREADY the topic)
Rule: If the THING is new → が. If the THING is already known → は.

Structure: [Place] に [Thing/Person] が あります / います

ParticleFunctionWhen to Use
はTopic (known info)When the THING is already known: ほんは つくえのうえに あります
がSubject (new info)When introducing something NEW: つくえのうえに ほんが あります

Example 1:
こうえんに こどもが います。
Kouen ni kodomo ga imasu.
There are children in the park. (new info)

Example 2:
かばんは いすのうえに あります。
Kaban wa isu no ue ni arimasu.
The bag is on the chair. (answering "where is the bag?")

⚠️ TRAP: は vs が here — Use が when INTRODUCING something ("there is a X"). Use は when the item is ALREADY the topic ("the X is at...").

G5: ね vs よ Sentence-Final Particles

What is it? These tiny particles go at the very END of a sentence and add emotional nuance. ね is like saying "...right?" — you are checking if the listener agrees. よ is like saying "I'm telling you!" — you are giving information the listener does NOT know yet.

Why: Using ね and よ correctly makes you sound natural. Misusing them can sound rude or uncertain.

🧠 Memory trick: ね = nod together ("...right?" — you both agree). よ = yo, listen up! ("Hey, I'm telling you something new!"). ね = shared knowledge. よ = one-way info delivery.
ParticleFunctionWhen to Use
ねSeeking agreement / confirmationWhen you think the listener AGREES: "...right?"
よGiving new information / assertingWhen the listener DOESN'T KNOW: "I tell you..."

Example 1:
いいてんきですね。
Ii tenki desu ne.
Nice weather, isn't it?

Example 2:
このレストランは おいしいですよ。
Kono resutoran wa oishii desu yo.
This restaurant is delicious, I'm telling you!

⚠️ TRAP: Using よ when the listener already knows something sounds condescending. Using ね when stating a fact the listener doesn't know sounds uncertain. Match the particle to the information status.
CH 5: ALL 5 GRAMMAR POINTS — COMPLETE BREAKDOWN

G1: この / その / あの / どの + Noun

What is it? In Ch 4, you learned これ/それ/あれ (this/that/that over there), which stand ALONE as pronouns. Now you learn their modifier versions: この/その/あの, which go BEFORE a noun. The difference is like "this" (alone) vs "this book" (before a noun). You cannot use この alone or これ before a noun.

Why: You need these to point at specific things: "this restaurant," "that person," "which car."

🔄 Don't confuse これ vs この:
• これは ほんです。 = This is a book. (これ alone, no noun after)
• このほんは おもしろいです。 = This book is interesting. (この + noun)
• これ ほん = WRONG. このは = WRONG.

Structure: [Demonstrative Adj] + [Noun]

Pronoun (standalone)Adjective (+ noun)Distance
これこの + nounNear speaker
それその + nounNear listener
あれあの + nounFar from both
どれどの + nounWhich?

Example 1:
このほんは いくらですか。
Kono hon wa ikura desu ka.
How much is this book?

Example 2:
あのひとは だれですか。
Ano hito wa dare desu ka.
Who is that person (over there)?

⚠️ TRAP: これ / それ / あれ / どれ stand ALONE (no noun after). この / その / あの / どの MUST have a noun after. WRONG: この は いいです. WRONG: これ ほん. RIGHT: この ほん or これは ほんです.

G2: Location Nouns (All 11)

What is it? These are words like "on top of," "under," "next to," "inside." In English, these are prepositions that go BEFORE the noun ("on the desk"). In Japanese, they are NOUNS that go AFTER the reference object, connected by の: つくえの うえに = desk's top-on = on the desk.

Why: You need these for any sentence about WHERE something is. Directions, room descriptions, lost items — all require location nouns.

🧠 Memory trick: The formula is always: [Thing] の [Location] に. Read it as "[Thing]'s [location] at." Example: つくえ の うえ に = desk's top at = on top of the desk. Always three pieces: reference + の + location word + に.

Structure: [Reference Noun] の [Location Noun] に

JapaneseRomajiMeaning
うえueon top of, above
したshitaunder, below
まえmaein front of
うしろushirobehind
みぎmigiright
ひだりhidarileft
なかnakainside
そとsotooutside
となりtonarinext to (same type)
よこyokobeside (different type)
ちかくchikakunear, vicinity

Example 1:
ほんは つくえのうえに あります。
Hon wa tsukue no ue ni arimasu.
The book is on the desk.

Example 2:
ぎんこうの となりに ゆうびんきょくが あります。
Ginkou no tonari ni yuubinkyoku ga arimasu.
There is a post office next to the bank.

⚠️ TRAP: となり vs よこ — となり = next to something of the SAME type (building next to building, person next to person). よこ = beside something of a DIFFERENT type (person beside a building). On exams, this distinction matters!

G3: Pronoun の

What is it? When a noun is obvious from context, you can replace it with の to avoid repeating yourself. It works like "one" in English: "the red one" instead of "the red umbrella." You can say わたしの (mine) instead of わたしの かさ (my umbrella) when it is clear you are talking about an umbrella.

Why: Natural Japanese avoids unnecessary repetition. Using pronoun の makes your speech sound more fluent and less robotic.

🧠 Memory trick: の = "the one." あかいの = the red one. わたしの = mine (= my one). おおきいの = the big one. It swallows the noun so you don't have to repeat it.

Structure: [Noun/Adj] の = replaces a noun already understood

TypeFull FormShort Form with の
Noun のわたしの かさわたしの (mine)
Adj のあかい かさあかいの (the red one)

Example 1:
このかさは わたしのです。
Kono kasa wa watashi no desu.
This umbrella is mine.

Example 2:
あかいのを ください。
Akai no wo kudasai.
Please give me the red one.

⚠️ TRAP: Pronoun の CANNOT follow この / その / あの / どの. WRONG: このの. To say "this one," use これ instead. RIGHT: あかいのは これです.

G4: Travel Pattern から / まで / で / ぐらい

What is it? This is the pattern for talking about travel: "From [A] to [B] by [transport] it takes about [duration]." Japanese has a specific particle for each slot. Once you memorize this formula, you can describe ANY journey in one sentence.

Why: You will use this for directions, commute discussions, travel planning — any time distance or travel time comes up.

🧠 Memory trick: The 5-slot formula: FROM-TO-BY-ABOUT-TAKES. In Japanese: から-まで-で-ぐらい-かかります. Just fill in each blank. Example: Osaka から Tokyo まで bullet train で 3 hours ぐらい かかります.
🔄 Don't confuse ごろ vs ぐらい:
• くじごろ = around 9:00 (a point in time)
• にじかんぐらい = about 2 hours (a duration/amount)
"Around WHEN?" = ごろ. "About HOW LONG?" = ぐらい.

5-Slot Formula:

[Place A] から [Place B] まで [Transport] で [Duration] ぐらい かかります

SlotParticleMeaningExample
Originからfromおおさかから
Destinationまでto / untilとうきょうまで
Transportでby means ofでんしゃで
Durationぐらいabout / approximatelyさんじかんぐらい
Verb(none)it takesかかります

Example 1:
おおさかから とうきょうまで しんかんせんで さんじかんぐらい かかります。
Oosaka kara Toukyou made shinkansen de sanjikan gurai kakarimasu.
It takes about 3 hours from Osaka to Tokyo by bullet train.

Example 2:
うちから がっこうまで あるいて じゅっぷんぐらい かかります。
Uchi kara gakkou made aruite juppun gurai kakarimasu.
It takes about 10 minutes walking from home to school.

⚠️ TRAP #1: ごろ vs ぐらい — ごろ = "around" a POINT in time (くじごろ = around 9:00). ぐらい = "about" a DURATION (にじかんぐらい = about 2 hours). Never swap them!
⚠️ TRAP #2: NO particle before かかります. WRONG: さんじかんが かかります. RIGHT: さんじかん かかります.
⚠️ TRAP #3: Walking = あるいて (て-form, NO で). WRONG: あるいてで. RIGHT: あるいて. All other transport uses で.

G5: は vs が + Double Particles

What is it? This is the deeper dive into は vs が. In short: は says "we're talking about THIS thing." が says "THIS SPECIFIC thing does the action." Also introduces double particles — where you can combine で/に with は/も to say things like "at school too" or "on Sundays (as for Sundays)."

Why: Mastering は vs が is often called the hardest part of Japanese grammar. This gives you the foundation. Double particles let you express nuanced ideas.

🧠 Memory trick: は = "Speaking of..." (topic marker, background info). が = "It's THIS that..." (spotlight on new/specific info). When answering だれが (WHO?), the answer always uses が because you're identifying a specific person.
は (Topic)が (Subject)
Marks what the sentence is ABOUTMarks WHO/WHAT does the action (new info)
わたしは がくせいです (As for me, I am a student)だれが きますか (WHO is coming?)

Double Particles

CombinationExampleMeaning
ではがっこうでは にほんごを はなしますAt school (topic), I speak Japanese
でもがっこうでも べんきょうしますAt school too, I study
にはにちようびには やすみますOn Sundays (topic), I rest
にもにちようびにも はたらきますOn Sundays too, I work

Example 1:
がっこうでは えいごを はなします。
Gakkou de wa eigo wo hanashimasu.
At school (as for school), I speak English.

Example 2:
どようびにも クラスが あります。
Doyoubi ni mo kurasu ga arimasu.
On Saturday too, there is class.

⚠️ TRAP: を NEVER combines with は or も. When you need to topicalize a direct object, は replaces を entirely. WRONG: コーヒーをは. RIGHT: コーヒーは のみます (As for coffee, I drink it). Similarly, コーヒーも のみます (I also drink coffee).
CH 6: ALL 5 GRAMMAR POINTS — COMPLETE BREAKDOWN

G1: と (Accompaniment, Connector) and に (Recipient, Purpose)

What is it? Two particles with multiple uses. と has two jobs: (1) "with [person]" and (2) "and" between nouns. に adds two NEW jobs here: (1) "to [person]" (recipient of an action like writing a letter) and (2) purpose of going somewhere (かいものに いきます = go shopping, literally "go for the purpose of shopping").

Why: と lets you talk about doing things with people and listing items. に for recipient and purpose appears in daily conversation constantly.

🔄 Don't confuse: と connects NOUNS, not verbs!
• コーヒーとケーキ = coffee and cake (nouns → OK)
• たべるとのむ = eat and drink (verbs → WRONG!)
• たべて、のみます = eat and drink (use て-form to chain verbs)
ParticleUsePatternExample
とWith (person)[Person] と [Verb]ともだちと いきます (go with a friend)
とAnd (nouns)[Noun] と [Noun]コーヒーと ケーキ (coffee and cake)
にRecipient[Person] に [Verb]ともだちに てがみを かきます (write a letter to a friend)
にPurpose[Activity] に いきますかいものに いきます (go shopping)

Example 1:
しゅうまつに ともだちと えいがを みました。
Shuumatsu ni tomodachi to eiga wo mimashita.
I watched a movie with a friend on the weekend.

Example 2:
はは に てがみを かきました。
Haha ni tegami wo kakimashita.
I wrote a letter to my mother.

⚠️ TRAP: と connects NOUNS only, never verbs. WRONG: たべると のみます (eat and drink). To connect verbs, use て-form: たべて のみます. Also, purpose に uses the verb stem: かいものに いきます (NOT かいものを いきます).

G2: Past Adjectives

What is it? In Ch 4 you learned present adjectives ("Tokyo is big"). Now you learn past adjectives ("Tokyo was big"). い-adjectives change their ending: drop い → add かったです. な-adjectives just swap です for でした. And of course, いい (good) is completely irregular: よかったです.

Why: You need past adjectives for reviews ("The movie was interesting"), memories ("The trip was fun"), and comparisons ("Last week was cold").

🧠 Memory trick for い-adj past: Drop い, add かった + です. Think: "ka-tta" sounds like "cut-a" — you CUT the い and add かった. For な-adj, the word doesn't change at all — just say でした instead of です.

い-Adjective Conjugation

TenseAffirmativeNegative
Non-past~い です~く ないです
Past~かったです~くなかったです

な-Adjective Conjugation

TenseAffirmativeNegative
Non-past~です~じゃありません
Past~でした~じゃありませんでした

いい (good) — Full Irregular Conjugation

TenseAffirmativeNegative
Non-pastいいですよくないです
Pastよかったですよくなかったです

Example 1:
きのうの パーティーは たのしかったです。
Kinou no paatii wa tanoshikatta desu.
Yesterday's party was fun.

Example 2:
ホテルは きれいでした。
Hoteru wa kirei deshita.
The hotel was clean/pretty.

⚠️ TRAP: いい NEVER uses い- stem in any conjugation except the plain affirmative. Past = よかったです (NOT いかったです). Neg past = よくなかったです (NOT いくなかったです). Every single form switches to よ-.

G3: て-Form Verbs (Sequential Actions, Requests)

What is it? The て-form is the Swiss Army knife of Japanese grammar. It has many uses, but in Ch 6 you learn two: (1) chaining actions ("I ate breakfast and then went to school") and (2) polite requests (てください = "please do..."). The て-form itself is NOT a complete sentence — it always connects to something else.

When NOT to use it: て-form cannot end a sentence by itself (in polite speech). It always needs a second verb or ください after it.

Why: The て-form is used in dozens of grammar patterns in later chapters. Learning it now unlocks everything from "please do X" to "is currently doing X" to "may I do X?" It is arguably the most important verb form in all of Japanese.

🧠 Memory trick (て-form song): Sing the u-verb endings to any tune:
"u, tsu, ru → tte! mu, bu, nu → nde! ku → ite! gu → ide! su → shite!"
Ru-verbs: just drop る and add て. Irregulars: する→して, くる→きて.
THE BIG EXCEPTION: いく → いって (NOT いいて). This WILL be on your exam.

Uses:

UsePatternExample
Sequential actions[Verb て], [Verb]あさごはんを たべて、がっこうに いきます
Polite request[Verb て] くださいまってください (Please wait)

て-Form Formation Rules

Dictionary Endingて-FormExample
~う / ~つ / ~る~ってあう → あって、まつ → まって
~む / ~ぶ / ~ぬ~んでよむ → よんで、あそぶ → あそんで
~く~いてかく → かいて
~ぐ~いでおよぐ → およいで
~す~してはなす → はなして
Ru-verbs~てたべる → たべて、みる → みて
Irregular—する → して、くる → きて

Example 1:
おんがくを きいて、ほんを よみました。
Ongaku wo kiite, hon wo yomimashita.
I listened to music and read a book.

Example 2:
ちょっと まってください。
Chotto matte kudasai.
Please wait a moment.

⚠️ TRAP: いきます (to go) is a MAJOR exception: いきます → いって (NOT いいて). It looks like a ~く verb (いく) which should become いいて, but it irregularly becomes いって. This is the single most-tested exception.

G4: て-Form Adjectives (Connecting Descriptions)

What is it? Just like verbs have a て-form to chain actions ("ate AND went"), adjectives have a て-form to chain descriptions ("cheap AND delicious"). For い-adj: drop い, add くて. For な-adj: add で. This lets you stack multiple descriptions in one sentence.

Why: Without this, you can only use one adjective per sentence. With it, you can say "This city is big, clean, and famous" all at once.

🧠 Memory trick: い-adj て-form: swap い for くて (sounds like "koo-teh"). な-adj て-form: just add で (the simplest one). And いい → よくて (same irregular よ- stem as always).
TypeFormationExample
い-adjDrop い → add くてやすい → やすくて
な-adjAdd でしずか → しずかで
NounAdd でにほんじん → にほんじんで

Example 1:
このレストランは やすくて おいしいです。
Kono resutoran wa yasukute oishii desu.
This restaurant is cheap and delicious.

Example 2:
きょうとは ゆうめいで きれいです。
Kyouto wa yuumei de kirei desu.
Kyoto is famous and pretty.

⚠️ TRAP: いい → よくて (NOT いくて). Same irregular よ- stem as all other いい conjugations. Example: てんきが よくて、さんぽしました (The weather was good, and I took a walk).

G5: ませんか / ましょう Invitations

What is it? Two ways to invite someone to do something. ませんか is a polite invitation ("Would you like to...?"). ましょう is a suggestion to do something together ("Let's...!"). Use ませんか when asking, ましょう when it is already decided or you are enthusiastically suggesting.

Why: You need these to make plans with people, invite friends to events, and respond to invitations — critical for social Japanese.

🧠 Memory trick: ませんか = "Won't you...?" (polite, giving the other person a choice). ましょう = "Let's do it!" (more assertive, assuming agreement). The soft decline ちょっと... literally means "a little..." but culturally means "that is difficult for me." Japanese rarely says "no" directly.
🔄 Don't confuse: How to decline politely
• いいえ、いきません。 = Too blunt! This sounds rude.
• ちょっと... あしたは ちょっと。 = Perfect. Soft, indirect, culturally appropriate.
In Japanese culture, directly refusing an invitation is considered impolite. ちょっと... + trailing off is the standard way to decline.
FormFunctionExample
~ませんかPolite invitation / suggestionえいがを みませんか (Would you like to watch a movie?)
~ましょうLet's ~ (suggesting together)いきましょう (Let's go!)

Accept / Decline Patterns

ResponseJapaneseEnglish
Acceptいいですね。That sounds good.
Acceptええ、いきましょう。Yes, let's go.
Soft declineちょっと…That's a bit... (implies difficulty)
Decline + reasonちょっと… きょうは いそがしいです。It's a bit... I'm busy today.

Example 1:
こんど いっしょに テニスを しませんか。 — いいですね。しましょう。
Kondo issho ni tenisu wo shimasen ka. — Ii desu ne. Shimashou.
Would you like to play tennis together sometime? — That sounds good. Let's do it.

Example 2:
あした えいがを みませんか。 — ちょっと… あしたは ちょっと。
Ashita eiga wo mimasen ka. — Chotto... ashita wa chotto.
Would you like to watch a movie tomorrow? — Tomorrow is a bit difficult...

⚠️ TRAP: ちょっと… is a SOFT decline. It does NOT mean "a little" here — it implies "that's difficult for me" without directly saying no. This is a key Japanese cultural communication pattern. Never explicitly say いいえ、いきません to decline an invitation — it is too blunt.

📚 COMPLETE REFERENCE TABLES

や、ゆ、よya, yu, yo—hiragana y-rowonly 3 chars1

わ、を、んwa, wo, n—hiragana w-row + nを = object marker particle1

Chapter 3: Daily Life & Verbs

漢字/かなRomajiPOSDefinitionNotesCh
朝 / あさasanmorning—3
昼 / ひるhirunnoon, daytime—3
夜 / よるyorunnight, evening—3
朝ご飯 / あさごはんasagohannbreakfast—3
昼ご飯 / ひるごはんhirugohannlunch—3
晩ご飯 / ばんごはんbangohanndinner—3
授業 / じゅぎょうjugyounclass (session)—3
宿題 / しゅくだいshukudainhomework—3
テストtesutontestkatakana loanword3

うちuchinhome, house—3
食べます / たべますtabemasuv2to eat—3
飲みます / のみますnomimasuv1to drink—3
行きます / いきますikimasuv1to go—3

新しい / あたらしいatarashiiい-adjnew—4
